# Aggressor volume imbalance | QuestDB

On this page

Calculate the imbalance between buy and sell aggressor volume to analyze order flow. The aggressor is the party that initiated the trade by crossing the spread.

Problem: Measure order flow imbalance[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/aggressor-volume-imbalance/#problem-measure-order-flow-imbalance "Direct link to Problem: Measure order flow imbalance")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You have trade data with a `side` column indicating the aggressor (buyer or seller), and want to measure the imbalance between buying and selling pressure.

Solution: Aggregate by side and calculate ratios[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/aggressor-volume-imbalance/#solution-aggregate-by-side-and-calculate-ratios "Direct link to Solution: Aggregate by side and calculate ratios")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Aggressor volume imbalance per symbol[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=WITH%20volumes%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20SUM(CASE%20WHEN%20side%20%3D%20%27buy%27%20THEN%20amount%20ELSE%200%20END)%20AS%20buy_volume%2C%0A%20%20%20%20SUM(CASE%20WHEN%20side%20%3D%20%27sell%27%20THEN%20amount%20ELSE%200%20END)%20AS%20sell_volume%0A%20%20FROM%20trades%0A%20%20WHERE%20timestamp%20IN%20%27%24yesterday%27%0A%20%20%20%20AND%20symbol%20IN%20(%27ETH-USDT%27%2C%20%27BTC-USDT%27%2C%20%27ETH-BTC%27)%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20buy_volume%2C%0A%20%20sell_volume%2C%0A%20%20((buy_volume%20-%20sell_volume)%3A%3Adouble%20%2F%20(buy_volume%20%2B%20sell_volume))%20*%20100%20AS%20imbalance%0AFROM%20volumes%3B&executeQuery=true)

    WITH volumes AS (  SELECT    symbol,    SUM(CASE WHEN side = 'buy' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS buy_volume,    SUM(CASE WHEN side = 'sell' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS sell_volume  FROM trades  WHERE timestamp IN '$yesterday'    AND symbol IN ('ETH-USDT', 'BTC-USDT', 'ETH-BTC'))SELECT  symbol,  buy_volume,  sell_volume,  ((buy_volume - sell_volume)::double / (buy_volume + sell_volume)) * 100 AS imbalanceFROM volumes;

The imbalance ranges from -100% (all sell) to +100% (all buy), with 0% indicating balanced flow.

Related documentation

*   [CASE expressions](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/case/)
    
*   [Aggregation functions](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/aggregation/)
    

*   [Problem: Measure order flow imbalance](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/aggressor-volume-imbalance/#problem-measure-order-flow-imbalance)
    
*   [Solution: Aggregate by side and calculate ratios](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/aggressor-volume-imbalance/#solution-aggregate-by-side-and-calculate-ratios)

---

# ATR (Average True Range) | QuestDB

On this page

Average True Range (ATR) measures market volatility by calculating the average of true ranges over a period. Unlike simple high-low range, true range accounts for gaps between periods, making it more accurate for volatile markets.

Problem[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/atr/#problem "Direct link to Problem")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You want to measure volatility to set appropriate stop-losses or position sizes. Simple high-low range misses overnight gaps, and standard deviation assumes normal distribution which markets don't follow.

Solution[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/atr/#solution "Direct link to Solution")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Calculate 14-period ATR[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=DECLARE%0A%20%20%40symbol%20%3A%3D%20%27EURUSD%27%2C%0A%20%20%40lookback%20%3A%3D%20%27%24now%20-%202d..%24now%27%0A%0AWITH%20with_prev%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20high%2C%0A%20%20%20%20low%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20lag(close)%20OVER%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp)%20AS%20prev_close%0A%20%20FROM%20market_data_ohlc_15m%0A%20%20WHERE%20symbol%20%3D%20%40symbol%0A%20%20%20%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%40lookback%0A)%2C%0Atrue_range%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20high%2C%0A%20%20%20%20low%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20greatest(%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20high%20-%20low%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20abs(high%20-%20prev_close)%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20abs(low%20-%20prev_close)%0A%20%20%20%20)%20AS%20tr%0A%20%20FROM%20with_prev%0A%20%20WHERE%20prev_close%20IS%20NOT%20NULL%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20round(close%2C%205)%20AS%20close%2C%0A%20%20round(tr%2C%206)%20AS%20true_range%2C%0A%20%20round(avg(tr%2C%20%27period%27%2C%2014)%20OVER%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp)%2C%206)%20AS%20atr%0AFROM%20true_range%0AORDER%20BY%20timestamp%3B&executeQuery=true)

    DECLARE  @symbol := 'EURUSD',  @lookback := '$now - 2d..$now'WITH with_prev AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    high,    low,    close,    lag(close) OVER (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp) AS prev_close  FROM market_data_ohlc_15m  WHERE symbol = @symbol    AND timestamp IN @lookback),true_range AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    high,    low,    close,    greatest(      high - low,      abs(high - prev_close),      abs(low - prev_close)    ) AS tr  FROM with_prev  WHERE prev_close IS NOT NULL)SELECT  timestamp,  symbol,  round(close, 5) AS close,  round(tr, 6) AS true_range,  round(avg(tr, 'period', 14) OVER (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp), 6) AS atrFROM true_rangeORDER BY timestamp;

The query:

1.  Gets previous close using `lag()` to detect gaps
2.  Calculates true range as the greatest of:
    *   Current high - current low (intraday range)
    *   |Current high - previous close| (gap up)
    *   |Current low - previous close| (gap down)
3.  Applies 14-period EMA smoothing to get ATR

Interpreting results[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/atr/#interpreting-results "Direct link to Interpreting results")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **High ATR**: Market is volatile, use wider stops
*   **Low ATR**: Market is quiet, can use tighter stops
*   **Rising ATR**: Volatility increasing, often during trends or breakouts
*   **Falling ATR**: Volatility decreasing, often during consolidation

Common uses[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/atr/#common-uses "Direct link to Common uses")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Stop-loss placement:**

    -- Stop at 2x ATR below entryentry_price - 2 * atr AS stop_loss

**Position sizing:**

    -- Risk 1% of account, sized by ATR(account_size * 0.01) / atr AS position_size

EMA vs Wilder's smoothing

This recipe uses standard EMA smoothing via `avg(value, 'period', 14)` where α = 2/(N+1). Wilder's original ATR uses α = 1/N, which is more gradual. For exact Wilder smoothing with a 14-period lookback, use `avg(value, 'period', 27)`. Most modern platforms offer both variants.

Related documentation

*   [EMA window function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/reference/#avg)
    
*   [lag() function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/reference/#lag)
    
*   [greatest() function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/numeric/#greatest)
    

*   [Problem](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/atr/#problem)
    
*   [Solution](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/atr/#solution)
    
*   [Interpreting results](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/atr/#interpreting-results)
    
*   [Common uses](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/atr/#common-uses)

---

# Cumulative product for random walk | QuestDB

On this page

Calculate the cumulative product of daily returns to simulate a stock's price path (random walk). This is useful for financial modeling, backtesting trading strategies, and portfolio analysis where you need to compound returns over time.

Problem: Compound daily returns[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/cumulative-product/#problem-compound-daily-returns "Direct link to Problem: Compound daily returns")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You have a table with daily returns for a stock and want to calculate the cumulative price starting from an initial value (e.g., $100). Each day's price is calculated by multiplying the previous price by `(1 + return)`.

For example, with these daily returns:

| Date | Daily Return (%) |
| --- | --- |
| 2024-09-05 | 2.00 |
| 2024-09-06 | \-1.00 |
| 2024-09-07 | 1.50 |
| 2024-09-08 | \-3.00 |

You want to calculate:

| Date | Daily Return (%) | Stock Price |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 2024-09-05 | 2.00 | 102.00 |
| 2024-09-06 | \-1.00 | 100.98 |
| 2024-09-07 | 1.50 | 102.49 |
| 2024-09-08 | \-3.00 | 99.42 |

Solution: Use logarithms to convert multiplication to addition[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/cumulative-product/#solution-use-logarithms-to-convert-multiplication-to-addition "Direct link to Solution: Use logarithms to convert multiplication to addition")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Use the mathematical identity: **exp(sum(ln(x))) = product(x)**

This converts the cumulative product into a cumulative sum, which window functions handle naturally:

Calculate cumulative product via logarithms

    WITH ln_values AS (    SELECT        date,        return,        SUM(ln(1 + return)) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS ln_value    FROM daily_returns)SELECT    date,    return,    100 * exp(ln_value) AS stock_priceFROM ln_values;

This query:

1.  Calculates `ln(1 + return)` for each day
2.  Uses a cumulative `SUM` window function to add up the logarithms
3.  Applies `exp()` to convert back to the product

How it works[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/cumulative-product/#how-it-works "Direct link to How it works")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The mathematical identity used here is:

    product(1 + r₁, 1 + r₂, ..., 1 + rₙ) = exp(sum(ln(1 + r₁), ln(1 + r₂), ..., ln(1 + rₙ)))

Breaking it down:

*   `ln(1 + return)` converts each multiplicative factor to an additive one
*   `SUM(...) OVER (ORDER BY date)` creates a cumulative sum
*   `exp(ln_value)` converts the cumulative sum back to a cumulative product
*   Multiply by 100 to apply the starting price of $100

Adapting to your data[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/cumulative-product/#adapting-to-your-data "Direct link to Adapting to your data")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can easily modify this pattern:

**Different starting price:**

    SELECT date, return, 1000 * exp(ln_value) AS stock_price  -- Start at $1000FROM ln_values;

**Different time granularity:**

    -- For hourly returnsWITH ln_values AS (    SELECT        timestamp,        return,        SUM(ln(1 + return)) OVER (ORDER BY timestamp) AS ln_value    FROM hourly_returns)SELECT timestamp, 100 * exp(ln_value) AS price FROM ln_values;

**Multiple assets:**

    WITH ln_values AS (    SELECT        date,        symbol,        return,        SUM(ln(1 + return)) OVER (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY date) AS ln_value    FROM daily_returns)SELECT    date,    symbol,    100 * exp(ln_value) AS stock_priceFROM ln_values;

Use Case: Monte Carlo Simulation

This pattern is essential for Monte Carlo simulations in finance. Generate random returns, apply this cumulative product calculation, and run thousands of iterations to model possible future price paths.

Related Documentation

*   [Window functions](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/syntax/)
    
*   [Mathematical functions](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/numeric/)
    
*   [SUM aggregate](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/aggregation/#sum)
    

*   [Problem: Compound daily returns](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/cumulative-product/#problem-compound-daily-returns)
    
*   [Solution: Use logarithms to convert multiplication to addition](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/cumulative-product/#solution-use-logarithms-to-convert-multiplication-to-addition)
    
*   [How it works](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/cumulative-product/#how-it-works)
    
*   [Adapting to your data](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/cumulative-product/#adapting-to-your-data)

---

# Liquidity comparison across instruments | QuestDB

On this page

Compare liquidity across instruments by calculating the effective spread at a given order size. The effective spread measures the actual cost of executing a round-trip trade (buy then sell) using Level 2 order book data.

The problem[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/liquidity-comparison/#the-problem "Direct link to The problem")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You have order book snapshots for multiple instruments and want to compare which ones offer better liquidity for a target order size. The quoted spread (best bid vs best ask) does not tell the full story. Larger orders eat through multiple price levels.

Solution: Use L2Price to calculate effective spread[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/liquidity-comparison/#solution-use-l2price-to-calculate-effective-spread "Direct link to Solution: Use L2Price to calculate effective spread")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`L2Price` calculates the average execution price when filling an order against multiple price levels. The effective spread is the difference between the buy and sell execution prices for a given size.

Compare effective spread across instruments[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=WITH%20latest_books%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%20timestamp%2C%20symbol%2C%20bids%2C%20asks%0A%20%20FROM%20market_data%0A%20%20WHERE%20timestamp%20IN%20%27%24today%27%0A%20%20LATEST%20ON%20timestamp%20PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20asks%5B2%5D%2C%20asks%5B1%5D)%20AS%20buy_price%2C%0A%20%20L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20bids%5B2%5D%2C%20bids%5B1%5D)%20AS%20sell_price%2C%0A%20%20L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20asks%5B2%5D%2C%20asks%5B1%5D)%20-%20L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20bids%5B2%5D%2C%20bids%5B1%5D)%20AS%20effective_spread%2C%0A%20%20(L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20asks%5B2%5D%2C%20asks%5B1%5D)%20-%20L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20bids%5B2%5D%2C%20bids%5B1%5D))%20%2F%0A%20%20%20%20((L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20asks%5B2%5D%2C%20asks%5B1%5D)%20%2B%20L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20bids%5B2%5D%2C%20bids%5B1%5D))%20%2F%202)%20*%2010_000%20AS%20spread_bps%0AFROM%20latest_books%0AORDER%20BY%20spread_bps%3B&executeQuery=true)

    WITH latest_books AS (  SELECT timestamp, symbol, bids, asks  FROM market_data  WHERE timestamp IN '$today'  LATEST ON timestamp PARTITION BY symbol)SELECT  symbol,  L2PRICE(100_000, asks[2], asks[1]) AS buy_price,  L2PRICE(100_000, bids[2], bids[1]) AS sell_price,  L2PRICE(100_000, asks[2], asks[1]) - L2PRICE(100_000, bids[2], bids[1]) AS effective_spread,  (L2PRICE(100_000, asks[2], asks[1]) - L2PRICE(100_000, bids[2], bids[1])) /    ((L2PRICE(100_000, asks[2], asks[1]) + L2PRICE(100_000, bids[2], bids[1])) / 2) * 10_000 AS spread_bpsFROM latest_booksORDER BY spread_bps;

This query:

1.  Gets the latest order book snapshot for each symbol using `LATEST ON`
2.  Calculates the average execution price for buying and selling 100,000 units
3.  Computes the effective spread in absolute terms and basis points
4.  Ranks instruments by liquidity (lowest spread = most liquid)

Effective spread over time[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/liquidity-comparison/#effective-spread-over-time "Direct link to Effective spread over time")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Track how liquidity changes throughout the trading day:

Effective spread time-series[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=SELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20last((L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20asks%5B2%5D%2C%20asks%5B1%5D)%20-%20L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20bids%5B2%5D%2C%20bids%5B1%5D))%20%2F%0A%20%20%20%20((L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20asks%5B2%5D%2C%20asks%5B1%5D)%20%2B%20L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20bids%5B2%5D%2C%20bids%5B1%5D))%20%2F%202))%20*%2010_000%20AS%20spread_bps%0AFROM%20market_data%0AWHERE%20timestamp%20IN%20%27%24today%27%0A%20%20AND%20symbol%20IN%20(%27EURUSD%27%2C%20%27GBPUSD%27%2C%20%27USDJPY%27)%0ASAMPLE%20BY%201h%0AORDER%20BY%20timestamp%2C%20symbol%3B&executeQuery=true)

    SELECT  timestamp,  symbol,  last((L2PRICE(100_000, asks[2], asks[1]) - L2PRICE(100_000, bids[2], bids[1])) /    ((L2PRICE(100_000, asks[2], asks[1]) + L2PRICE(100_000, bids[2], bids[1])) / 2)) * 10_000 AS spread_bpsFROM market_dataWHERE timestamp IN '$today'  AND symbol IN ('EURUSD', 'GBPUSD', 'USDJPY')SAMPLE BY 1hORDER BY timestamp, symbol;

Compare liquidity at different order sizes[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/liquidity-comparison/#compare-liquidity-at-different-order-sizes "Direct link to Compare liquidity at different order sizes")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

See how execution costs scale with order size:

Liquidity depth analysis[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=WITH%20latest_books%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%20symbol%2C%20bids%2C%20asks%0A%20%20FROM%20market_data%0A%20%20WHERE%20timestamp%20IN%20%27%24today%27%0A%20%20LATEST%20ON%20timestamp%20PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20L2PRICE(10_000%2C%20asks%5B2%5D%2C%20asks%5B1%5D)%20-%20L2PRICE(10_000%2C%20bids%5B2%5D%2C%20bids%5B1%5D)%20AS%20spread_10k%2C%0A%20%20L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20asks%5B2%5D%2C%20asks%5B1%5D)%20-%20L2PRICE(100_000%2C%20bids%5B2%5D%2C%20bids%5B1%5D)%20AS%20spread_100k%2C%0A%20%20L2PRICE(500_000%2C%20asks%5B2%5D%2C%20asks%5B1%5D)%20-%20L2PRICE(500_000%2C%20bids%5B2%5D%2C%20bids%5B1%5D)%20AS%20spread_500k%2C%0A%20%20L2PRICE(1_000_000%2C%20asks%5B2%5D%2C%20asks%5B1%5D)%20-%20L2PRICE(1_000_000%2C%20bids%5B2%5D%2C%20bids%5B1%5D)%20AS%20spread_1m%0AFROM%20latest_books%0AORDER%20BY%20symbol%3B&executeQuery=true)

    WITH latest_books AS (  SELECT symbol, bids, asks  FROM market_data  WHERE timestamp IN '$today'  LATEST ON timestamp PARTITION BY symbol)SELECT  symbol,  L2PRICE(10_000, asks[2], asks[1]) - L2PRICE(10_000, bids[2], bids[1]) AS spread_10k,  L2PRICE(100_000, asks[2], asks[1]) - L2PRICE(100_000, bids[2], bids[1]) AS spread_100k,  L2PRICE(500_000, asks[2], asks[1]) - L2PRICE(500_000, bids[2], bids[1]) AS spread_500k,  L2PRICE(1_000_000, asks[2], asks[1]) - L2PRICE(1_000_000, bids[2], bids[1]) AS spread_1mFROM latest_booksORDER BY symbol;

Instruments with similar spreads across sizes have deeper liquidity.

Interpreting results[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/liquidity-comparison/#interpreting-results "Direct link to Interpreting results")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Lower spread\_bps** = better liquidity, lower trading costs
*   **Spread widening with size** = shallow order book, higher market impact
*   **NULL values** = insufficient liquidity to fill the target size

Related documentation

*   [L2Price function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/finance/#l2price)
    
*   [LATEST ON](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/latest-on/)

# Bollinger BandWidth | QuestDB

On this page

Bollinger BandWidth quantifies the width of [Bollinger Bands](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bands/)
 as a percentage, helping traders identify low-volatility squeeze conditions that often precede significant price moves.

Problem[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bandwidth/#problem "Direct link to Problem")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You have Bollinger Bands but want to objectively measure when volatility is unusually low. Visually spotting a "squeeze" is subjective. BandWidth provides a numeric value you can compare against historical levels to identify when bands are at their historical lows.

What is BandWidth?[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bandwidth/#what-is-bandwidth "Direct link to What is BandWidth?")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

BandWidth measures the percentage difference between the upper and lower Bollinger Bands:

    BandWidth = ((Upper Band - Lower Band) / Middle Band) × 100

When BandWidth drops to historically low levels, the bands are in a "squeeze". Periods of low volatility are often followed by high volatility, so a squeeze suggests a significant price move may be coming. The squeeze does not indicate direction, only that a breakout is likely.

Solution[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bandwidth/#solution "Direct link to Solution")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Calculate Bollinger BandWidth with range position[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=DECLARE%0A%20%20%40symbol%20%3A%3D%20%27BTC-USDT%27%2C%0A%20%20%40history%20%3A%3D%20%27%24now%20-%206M..%24now%27%2C%0A%20%20%40display%20%3A%3D%20%27%24now%20-%201M..%24now%27%0A%0AWITH%20daily_ohlc%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20first(open)%20AS%20open%2C%0A%20%20%20%20max(high)%20AS%20high%2C%0A%20%20%20%20min(low)%20AS%20low%2C%0A%20%20%20%20last(close)%20AS%20close%0A%20%20FROM%20trades_ohlc_15m%0A%20%20WHERE%20symbol%20%3D%20%40symbol%0A%20%20%20%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%40history%0A%20%20SAMPLE%20BY%201d%0A)%2C%0Abands%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20AVG(close)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20sma20%2C%0A%20%20%20%20AVG(close%20*%20close)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20avg_close_sq%0A%20%20FROM%20daily_ohlc%0A%20%20WINDOW%20w%20AS%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp%20ROWS%2019%20PRECEDING)%0A)%2C%0Abollinger%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20sma20%2C%0A%20%20%20%20sma20%20%2B%202%20*%20sqrt(avg_close_sq%20-%20(sma20%20*%20sma20))%20AS%20upper_band%2C%0A%20%20%20%20sma20%20-%202%20*%20sqrt(avg_close_sq%20-%20(sma20%20*%20sma20))%20AS%20lower_band%0A%20%20FROM%20bands%0A)%2C%0Awith_bandwidth%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20sma20%2C%0A%20%20%20%20upper_band%2C%0A%20%20%20%20lower_band%2C%0A%20%20%20%20(upper_band%20-%20lower_band)%20%2F%20sma20%20*%20100%20AS%20bandwidth%0A%20%20FROM%20bollinger%0A)%2C%0Awith_range%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20sma20%2C%0A%20%20%20%20upper_band%2C%0A%20%20%20%20lower_band%2C%0A%20%20%20%20bandwidth%2C%0A%20%20%20%20min(bandwidth)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20min_bw%2C%0A%20%20%20%20max(bandwidth)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20max_bw%0A%20%20FROM%20with_bandwidth%0A%20%20WINDOW%20w%20AS%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol)%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20round(close%2C%202)%20AS%20close%2C%0A%20%20round(sma20%2C%202)%20AS%20sma20%2C%0A%20%20round(upper_band%2C%202)%20AS%20upper_band%2C%0A%20%20round(lower_band%2C%202)%20AS%20lower_band%2C%0A%20%20round(bandwidth%2C%204)%20AS%20bandwidth%2C%0A%20%20round((bandwidth%20-%20min_bw)%20%2F%20(max_bw%20-%20min_bw)%20*%20100%2C%201)%20AS%20range_position%0AFROM%20with_range%0AWHERE%20timestamp%20IN%20%40display%0AORDER%20BY%20timestamp%3B&executeQuery=true)

    DECLARE  @symbol := 'BTC-USDT',  @history := '$now - 6M..$now',  @display := '$now - 1M..$now'WITH daily_ohlc AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    first(open) AS open,    max(high) AS high,    min(low) AS low,    last(close) AS close  FROM trades_ohlc_15m  WHERE symbol = @symbol    AND timestamp IN @history  SAMPLE BY 1d),bands AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    close,    AVG(close) OVER w AS sma20,    AVG(close * close) OVER w AS avg_close_sq  FROM daily_ohlc  WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp ROWS 19 PRECEDING)),bollinger AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    close,    sma20,    sma20 + 2 * sqrt(avg_close_sq - (sma20 * sma20)) AS upper_band,    sma20 - 2 * sqrt(avg_close_sq - (sma20 * sma20)) AS lower_band  FROM bands),with_bandwidth AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    close,    sma20,    upper_band,    lower_band,    (upper_band - lower_band) / sma20 * 100 AS bandwidth  FROM bollinger),with_range AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    close,    sma20,    upper_band,    lower_band,    bandwidth,    min(bandwidth) OVER w AS min_bw,    max(bandwidth) OVER w AS max_bw  FROM with_bandwidth  WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY symbol))SELECT  timestamp,  symbol,  round(close, 2) AS close,  round(sma20, 2) AS sma20,  round(upper_band, 2) AS upper_band,  round(lower_band, 2) AS lower_band,  round(bandwidth, 4) AS bandwidth,  round((bandwidth - min_bw) / (max_bw - min_bw) * 100, 1) AS range_positionFROM with_rangeWHERE timestamp IN @displayORDER BY timestamp;

The query first aggregates 15-minute candles into daily OHLC, then calculates standard 20-day Bollinger Bands. This matches the traditional approach where SMA20 represents roughly one month of trading. The 6-month lookback (`@history`) establishes the historical range, while `@display` limits output to the last month. Standard deviation uses the variance formula `sqrt(avg(x²) - avg(x)²)`.

The `range_position` shows where current BandWidth falls within the 6-month range: 0% means at the historical minimum, 100% at the maximum. This works well for identifying squeeze conditions since you're comparing against historical extremes.

Interpreting results[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bandwidth/#interpreting-results "Direct link to Interpreting results")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Low range position** (< 20%): BandWidth is near 6-month lows, indicating a squeeze
*   **High range position** (> 80%): BandWidth is near 6-month highs, volatility is elevated
*   **Rising BandWidth**: Volatility increasing, bands expanding
*   **Falling BandWidth**: Volatility decreasing, bands contracting

A squeeze signals that volatility expansion is likely, but not the direction. Use price action or other indicators to determine breakout direction.

Related documentation

*   [Bollinger Bands recipe](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bands/)
    
*   [Window functions](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/overview/)
    

*   [Problem](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bandwidth/#problem)
    
*   [What is BandWidth?](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bandwidth/#what-is-bandwidth)
    
*   [Solution](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bandwidth/#solution)
    
*   [Interpreting results](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bandwidth/#interpreting-results)

---

# Gamma scalping signal | QuestDB

On this page

The vol-spread ratio compares realized price movement against the cost of crossing the bid-ask spread. A high ratio means the market is moving significantly relative to transaction costs; a low ratio means spread costs will likely eat into rebalancing profits. For background on the strategy itself, see the [gamma scalping strategies](https://questdb.com/glossary/gamma-scalping-strategies/)
 glossary entry.

Problem[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/gamma-scalping-signal/#problem "Direct link to Problem")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You want to assess whether market conditions favor gamma scalping by comparing realized price movement against the cost of crossing the bid-ask spread.

Solution[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/gamma-scalping-signal/#solution "Direct link to Solution")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Gamma scalping signal: vol-spread ratio[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=DECLARE%0A%20%20%40symbol%20%3A%3D%20%27EURUSD%27%2C%0A%20%20%40lookback%20%3A%3D%20%27%24now%20-%201d..%24now%27%0A%0AWITH%20sampled%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20last((bid_price%20%2B%20ask_price)%20%2F%202)%20AS%20close_mid%2C%0A%20%20%20%20avg((ask_price%20-%20bid_price)%20%2F%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20((bid_price%20%2B%20ask_price)%20%2F%202))%20AS%20avg_rel_spread%0A%20%20FROM%20core_price%0A%20%20WHERE%20symbol%20%3D%20%40symbol%0A%20%20%20%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%40lookback%0A%20%20SAMPLE%20BY%201m%20ALIGN%20TO%20CALENDAR%0A)%2C%0Areturns%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close_mid%2C%0A%20%20%20%20avg_rel_spread%2C%0A%20%20%20%20LN(close_mid%20%2F%20LAG(close_mid)%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20OVER%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp))%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20AS%20log_ret%0A%20%20FROM%20sampled%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20round(AVG(avg_rel_spread)%20OVER%20w%20*%2010000%2C%202)%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20AS%20rel_spread_1h_bps%2C%0A%20%20round(SQRT(%0A%20%20%20%20(AVG(log_ret%20*%20log_ret)%20OVER%20w%20-%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20AVG(log_ret)%20OVER%20w%20*%20AVG(log_ret)%20OVER%20w)%20*%201440%20*%20365%0A%20%20)%20*%20100%2C%202)%20AS%20realized_vol_1h_ann%2C%0A%20%20CASE%0A%20%20%20%20WHEN%20AVG(avg_rel_spread)%20OVER%20w%20%3E%200%20THEN%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20round(SQRT(%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20(AVG(log_ret%20*%20log_ret)%20OVER%20w%20-%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20AVG(log_ret)%20OVER%20w%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20*%20AVG(log_ret)%20OVER%20w)%20*%201440%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20)%20%2F%20AVG(avg_rel_spread)%20OVER%20w%2C%202)%0A%20%20%20%20ELSE%20NULL%0A%20%20END%20AS%20vol_spread_ratio%0AFROM%20returns%0AWHERE%20log_ret%20IS%20NOT%20NULL%0AWINDOW%20w%20AS%20(%0A%20%20PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp%20ROWS%2059%20PRECEDING%0A)%3B&executeQuery=true)

    DECLARE  @symbol := 'EURUSD',  @lookback := '$now - 1d..$now'WITH sampled AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    last((bid_price + ask_price) / 2) AS close_mid,    avg((ask_price - bid_price) /        ((bid_price + ask_price) / 2)) AS avg_rel_spread  FROM core_price  WHERE symbol = @symbol    AND timestamp IN @lookback  SAMPLE BY 1m ALIGN TO CALENDAR),returns AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    close_mid,    avg_rel_spread,    LN(close_mid / LAG(close_mid)        OVER (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp))        AS log_ret  FROM sampled)SELECT  timestamp,  symbol,  round(AVG(avg_rel_spread) OVER w * 10000, 2)      AS rel_spread_1h_bps,  round(SQRT(    (AVG(log_ret * log_ret) OVER w -        AVG(log_ret) OVER w * AVG(log_ret) OVER w) * 1440 * 365  ) * 100, 2) AS realized_vol_1h_ann,  CASE    WHEN AVG(avg_rel_spread) OVER w > 0 THEN      round(SQRT(        (AVG(log_ret * log_ret) OVER w -            AVG(log_ret) OVER w            * AVG(log_ret) OVER w) * 1440      ) / AVG(avg_rel_spread) OVER w, 2)    ELSE NULL  END AS vol_spread_ratioFROM returnsWHERE log_ret IS NOT NULLWINDOW w AS (  PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp ROWS 59 PRECEDING);

How it works[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/gamma-scalping-signal/#how-it-works "Direct link to How it works")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Gamma scalping is an options strategy where traders buy a straddle or strangle and continuously rebalance by buying and selling the underlying as the price moves. Each rebalance crosses the bid-ask spread. The strategy only works if the market moves enough for the scalping profits to exceed two costs: theta decay (the daily loss from holding the options) and the spread cost of each rebalancing trade.

This recipe addresses the spread cost side. The `vol_spread_ratio` measures how much the mid-price is moving per unit of transaction cost over a rolling one-hour window:

*   `realized_vol_1h_ann` is the annualized realized volatility, computed from one-minute log returns using the same method as the [realized volatility](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/realized-volatility/)
     recipe
*   `rel_spread_1h_bps` is the average relative bid-ask spread over the same rolling hour, expressed in basis points. Using relative spread (spread divided by mid-price) makes it dimensionless, matching the units of log-return volatility
*   `vol_spread_ratio` divides the non-annualized daily volatility by the average relative spread - both are dimensionless, so the ratio is a pure number. Higher values mean more price movement per unit of spread cost

The query computes both the spread and the mid-price in the same `SAMPLE BY` pass, so the spread is the average across all ticks within each one-minute bar, not just the closing snapshot.

The named `WINDOW w` clause avoids repeating the frame definition, which is important for readability given three output columns sharing the same window.

When interpreting the ratio: if it is consistently low (quiet market, wide spreads, or both), gamma scalping is unlikely to cover its spread costs. Periods of elevated ratio, often around economic data releases or session overlaps, indicate favorable conditions for rebalancing. However, a high vol-spread ratio is necessary but not sufficient - the strategy must also overcome theta decay (the daily cost of holding the options), which depends on implied volatility and option pricing. This recipe only addresses the spread cost side.

Annualization factor

The demo FX data is simulated continuously (24/7, including weekends), so the annualization factor uses `1440 * 365` (1,440 minutes per day times 365 days per year). For real FX markets (24/5), use `1440 * 260` (see the [realized volatility](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/realized-volatility/)
 recipe for why FX uses 260 rather than 252). The `vol_spread_ratio` uses non-annualized daily volatility (factor `1440`, i.e. minutes per day, without yearly scaling) since both numerator and denominator are dimensionless.

Related documentation

*   [Realized Volatility](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/realized-volatility/)
     - the volatility component of this recipe
*   [Log returns](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/log-returns/)
     - the building block for realized volatility
*   [Bid-Ask Spread](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bid-ask-spread/)
     - the spread component of this recipe
*   [Liquidity Comparison](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/liquidity-comparison/)
     - related microstructure analysis

*   [Problem](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/gamma-scalping-signal/#problem)
    
*   [Solution](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/gamma-scalping-signal/#solution)
    
*   [How it works](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/gamma-scalping-signal/#how-it-works)

---

# Donchian Channels | QuestDB

On this page

Donchian Channels plot the highest high and lowest low over a period, creating a channel that tracks price range. Breakouts above the upper channel or below the lower channel often signal trend continuation.

Problem[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/donchian-channels/#problem "Direct link to Problem")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You want to identify breakout levels and trading ranges. Moving averages smooth price but don't show clear breakout levels. Donchian Channels show exactly where price needs to go to break out of its recent range.

Solution[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/donchian-channels/#solution "Direct link to Solution")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Calculate 20-period Donchian Channels[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=DECLARE%0A%20%20%40symbol%20%3A%3D%20%27EURUSD%27%2C%0A%20%20%40lookback%20%3A%3D%20%27%24now%20-%202d..%24now%27%0A%0AWITH%20channels%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20max(high)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20upper_channel%2C%0A%20%20%20%20min(low)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20lower_channel%0A%20%20FROM%20market_data_ohlc_15m%0A%20%20WHERE%20symbol%20%3D%20%40symbol%0A%20%20%20%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%40lookback%0A%20%20WINDOW%20w%20AS%20(%0A%20%20%20%20PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp%0A%20%20%20%20ROWS%20BETWEEN%2019%20PRECEDING%20AND%20CURRENT%20ROW%0A%20%20)%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20round(close%2C%205)%20AS%20close%2C%0A%20%20round(upper_channel%2C%205)%20AS%20upper_channel%2C%0A%20%20round(lower_channel%2C%205)%20AS%20lower_channel%2C%0A%20%20round((upper_channel%20%2B%20lower_channel)%20%2F%202%2C%205)%20AS%20middle_channel%0AFROM%20channels%0AORDER%20BY%20timestamp%3B&executeQuery=true)

    DECLARE  @symbol := 'EURUSD',  @lookback := '$now - 2d..$now'WITH channels AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    close,    max(high) OVER w AS upper_channel,    min(low) OVER w AS lower_channel  FROM market_data_ohlc_15m  WHERE symbol = @symbol    AND timestamp IN @lookback  WINDOW w AS (    PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp    ROWS BETWEEN 19 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW  ))SELECT  timestamp,  symbol,  round(close, 5) AS close,  round(upper_channel, 5) AS upper_channel,  round(lower_channel, 5) AS lower_channel,  round((upper_channel + lower_channel) / 2, 5) AS middle_channelFROM channelsORDER BY timestamp;

The query calculates:

*   **Upper channel**: 20-period highest high
*   **Lower channel**: 20-period lowest low
*   **Middle channel**: Average of upper and lower

Interpreting results[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/donchian-channels/#interpreting-results "Direct link to Interpreting results")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Price breaks above upper**: Bullish breakout, potential long entry
*   **Price breaks below lower**: Bearish breakout, potential short entry
*   **Price at middle**: Neutral zone
*   **Narrow channel**: Low volatility, breakout likely coming
*   **Wide channel**: High volatility, trend in progress

Turtle Trading

Donchian Channels were famously used by the Turtle Traders. Their system entered on 20-day breakouts and exited on 10-day breakouts in the opposite direction.

Related documentation

*   [min/max window functions](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/reference/#min)
    
*   [Window functions overview](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/overview/)
    

*   [Problem](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/donchian-channels/#problem)
    
*   [Solution](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/donchian-channels/#solution)
    
*   [Interpreting results](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/donchian-channels/#interpreting-results)

---

# Bollinger bands | QuestDB

On this page

Calculate Bollinger Bands for volatility analysis and mean reversion trading. Bollinger Bands consist of a moving average with upper and lower bands set at a specified number of standard deviations above and below it. They help identify overbought/oversold conditions and measure market volatility.

Measuring band width

To objectively measure when bands are contracting (a "squeeze"), see the [Bollinger BandWidth recipe](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bandwidth/)
 which calculates band width as a percentage and compares it to historical levels.

note

Bollinger Bands can be calculated using either population standard deviation (stddev) or sample standard deviation (stddev\_samp), producing slightly different results. This recipe uses stddev.

Solution: Calculate variance using window functions[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bands/#solution-calculate-variance-using-window-functions "Direct link to Solution: Calculate variance using window functions")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Since standard deviation is the square root of variance, and variance is the average of squared differences from the mean, we can calculate everything in SQL using window functions. This query will compute Bollinger Bands with a 20-period simple moving average (SMA) and bands at ±2 standard deviations:

Calculate Bollinger Bands with 20-period SMA[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=WITH%20OHLC%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20first(price)%20AS%20open%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20max(price)%20as%20high%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20min(price)%20as%20low%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20last(price)%20AS%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20sum(quantity)%20AS%20volume%0A%20FROM%20fx_trades%0A%20WHERE%20symbol%20%3D%20%27EURUSD%27%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%27%24yesterday%27%0A%20SAMPLE%20BY%2015m%0A)%2C%20stats%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20AVG(close)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20sma20%2C%0A%20%20%20%20AVG(close%20*%20close)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20avg_close_sq%0A%20%20FROM%20OHLC%0A%20%20WINDOW%20w%20AS%20(ORDER%20BY%20timestamp%20ROWS%2019%20PRECEDING)%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20sma20%2C%0A%20%20sqrt(avg_close_sq%20-%20(sma20%20*%20sma20))%20as%20stdev20%2C%0A%20%20sma20%20%2B%202%20*%20sqrt(avg_close_sq%20-%20(sma20%20*%20sma20))%20as%20upper_band%2C%0A%20%20sma20%20-%202%20*%20sqrt(avg_close_sq%20-%20(sma20%20*%20sma20))%20as%20lower_band%0AFROM%20stats%0AORDER%20BY%20timestamp%3B&executeQuery=true)

    WITH OHLC AS (  SELECT    timestamp, symbol,      first(price) AS open,      max(price) as high,      min(price) as low,      last(price) AS close,      sum(quantity) AS volume FROM fx_trades WHERE symbol = 'EURUSD' AND timestamp IN '$yesterday' SAMPLE BY 15m), stats AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    close,    AVG(close) OVER w AS sma20,    AVG(close * close) OVER w AS avg_close_sq  FROM OHLC  WINDOW w AS (ORDER BY timestamp ROWS 19 PRECEDING))SELECT  timestamp,  close,  sma20,  sqrt(avg_close_sq - (sma20 * sma20)) as stdev20,  sma20 + 2 * sqrt(avg_close_sq - (sma20 * sma20)) as upper_band,  sma20 - 2 * sqrt(avg_close_sq - (sma20 * sma20)) as lower_bandFROM statsORDER BY timestamp;

This query:

1.  Aggregates trades into 15-minute OHLC candles
2.  Calculates a 20-period simple moving average of closing prices
3.  Calculates the average of squared closing prices over the same 20-period window
4.  Computes standard deviation using the mathematical identity: `σ = √(E[X²] - E[X]²)`
5.  Adds/subtracts 2× standard deviation to create upper and lower bands

How it works[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bands/#how-it-works "Direct link to How it works")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The core of the Bollinger Bands calculation is the rolling standard deviation. Please check our [rolling standard deviation recipe](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/rolling-stddev/)
 in the cookbook for an explanation about the mathematical formula.

Adapting the parameters[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bands/#adapting-the-parameters "Direct link to Adapting the parameters")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Different period lengths:**

    -- 10-period Bollinger Bands (change ROWS 19 to ROWS 9)AVG(close) OVER w AS sma10,AVG(close * close) OVER w AS avg_close_sq...WINDOW w AS (ORDER BY timestamp ROWS 9 PRECEDING)

**Different band multipliers:**

    -- 1 standard deviation bands (tighter)sma20 + 1 * sqrt(avg_close_sq - (sma20 * sma20)) as upper_band,sma20 - 1 * sqrt(avg_close_sq - (sma20 * sma20)) as lower_band-- 3 standard deviation bands (wider)sma20 + 3 * sqrt(avg_close_sq - (sma20 * sma20)) as upper_band,sma20 - 3 * sqrt(avg_close_sq - (sma20 * sma20)) as lower_band

**Different time intervals:**

    -- 5-minute candlesSAMPLE BY 5m-- 1-hour candlesSAMPLE BY 1h

**Multiple symbols:**

Bollinger Bands for multiple symbols[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=WITH%20OHLC%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20first(price)%20AS%20open%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20last(price)%20AS%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20sum(quantity)%20AS%20volume%0A%20FROM%20fx_trades%0A%20WHERE%20symbol%20IN%20(%27EURUSD%27%2C%20%27GBPUSD%27)%0A%20%20%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%27%24yesterday%27%0A%20SAMPLE%20BY%2015m%0A)%2C%20stats%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20AVG(close)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20sma20%2C%0A%20%20%20%20AVG(close%20*%20close)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20avg_close_sq%0A%20%20FROM%20OHLC%0A%20%20WINDOW%20w%20AS%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp%20ROWS%2019%20PRECEDING)%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20sma20%2C%0A%20%20sma20%20%2B%202%20*%20sqrt(avg_close_sq%20-%20(sma20%20*%20sma20))%20as%20upper_band%2C%0A%20%20sma20%20-%202%20*%20sqrt(avg_close_sq%20-%20(sma20%20*%20sma20))%20as%20lower_band%0AFROM%20stats%0AORDER%20BY%20symbol%2C%20timestamp%3B&executeQuery=true)

    WITH OHLC AS (  SELECT    timestamp, symbol,      first(price) AS open,      last(price) AS close,      sum(quantity) AS volume FROM fx_trades WHERE symbol IN ('EURUSD', 'GBPUSD')   AND timestamp IN '$yesterday' SAMPLE BY 15m), stats AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    close,    AVG(close) OVER w AS sma20,    AVG(close * close) OVER w AS avg_close_sq  FROM OHLC  WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp ROWS 19 PRECEDING))SELECT  timestamp,  symbol,  close,  sma20,  sma20 + 2 * sqrt(avg_close_sq - (sma20 * sma20)) as upper_band,  sma20 - 2 * sqrt(avg_close_sq - (sma20 * sma20)) as lower_bandFROM statsORDER BY symbol, timestamp;

Note the addition of `PARTITION BY symbol` to calculate separate Bollinger Bands for each symbol.

Related documentation

*   [Bollinger BandWidth recipe](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bandwidth/)
    
*   [Window functions](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/syntax/)
    
*   [AVG window function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/reference/#avg)
    
*   [SQRT function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/numeric/#sqrt)
    
*   [Window frame clauses](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/syntax/#frame-types-and-behavior)
    

*   [Solution: Calculate variance using window functions](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bands/#solution-calculate-variance-using-window-functions)
    
*   [How it works](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bands/#how-it-works)
    
*   [Adapting the parameters](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bands/#adapting-the-parameters)

# Comparison Operators | QuestDB

On this page

This page describes the available operators to assist with comparison operations.

If `string` or `char` values are used in the input, they are converted to `int` using the [ASCII Table](https://www.asciitable.com/)
 for comparison.

`IN` (list)[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#in-list "Direct link to in-list")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`X IN (a, b, c)` returns true if X is present in the list.

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#example "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT 5 IN (1, 2, 7, 5, 8)

| column |
| --- |
| true |

`=` Equals[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-equals "Direct link to -equals")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`(value1) = (value2)` - returns true if the two values are the same.

#### Arguments[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#arguments "Direct link to Arguments")

*   `value1` is any data type.
*   `value2` is any data type.

#### Return value[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#return-value "Direct link to Return value")

Return value type is boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#examples "Direct link to Examples")

    SELECT '5' = '5';-- Returns trueSELECT 5 = 5;-- Returns trueSELECT '5' = '3';-- Returns falseSELECT 5 = 3;-- Returns false

`>` Greater than[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-greater-than "Direct link to -greater-than")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   `(value1) > (value2)` - returns true if `value1` is greater than `value2`.

#### Arguments[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#arguments-1 "Direct link to Arguments")

*   `value1` and `value2` are one of the following data types:
    *   any numeric data type
    *   `char`
    *   `date`
    *   `timestamp`
    *   `symbol`
    *   `string`

#### Return value[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#return-value-1 "Direct link to Return value")

Return value type is boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#examples-1 "Direct link to Examples")

    SELECT 'abc' > 'def';-- Returns falseSELECT '5' > '5';-- Returns falseSELECT 'a' > 'b';-- Returns false

`>=` Greater than or equal to[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-greater-than-or-equal-to "Direct link to -greater-than-or-equal-to")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   `(value1) >= (value2)` - returns true if `value1` is greater than `value2`.

#### Arguments[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#arguments-2 "Direct link to Arguments")

*   `value1` and `value2` are one of the following data types:
    *   any numeric data type
    *   `char`
    *   `date`
    *   `timestamp`
    *   `symbol`
    *   `string`

#### Return value[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#return-value-2 "Direct link to Return value")

Return value type is boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#examples-2 "Direct link to Examples")

    SELECT 'abc' >= 'def';-- Returns falseSELECT '5' >= '5';-- Returns trueSELECT '7' >= '5';-- Returns trueSELECT 'a' >= 'b';-- Returns false

`<` Lesser than[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-lesser-than "Direct link to -lesser-than")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   `(value1) < (value2)` - returns true if `value1` is less than `value2`.

#### Arguments[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#arguments-3 "Direct link to Arguments")

*   `value1` and `value2` are one of the following data types:
    *   any numeric data type
    *   `char`
    *   `date`
    *   `timestamp`
    *   `symbol`
    *   `string`

#### Return value[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#return-value-3 "Direct link to Return value")

Return value type is boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#examples-3 "Direct link to Examples")

    SELECT '123' < '456';-- Returns trueSELECT 5 < 5;-- Returns falseSELECT 5 < 3;-- Returns false

`<=` Lesser than or equal to[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-lesser-than-or-equal-to "Direct link to -lesser-than-or-equal-to")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   `(value1) <= (value2)` - returns true if `value1` is less than `value2`.

#### Arguments[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#arguments-4 "Direct link to Arguments")

*   `value1` and `value2` are one of the following data types:
    *   any numeric data type
    *   `char`
    *   `date`
    *   `timestamp`
    *   `symbol`
    *   `string`

#### Return value[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#return-value-4 "Direct link to Return value")

Return value type is boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#examples-4 "Direct link to Examples")

    SELECT '123' <= '456';-- Returns trueSELECT 5 <= 5;-- Returns trueSELECT 5 <= 3;-- Returns false

`<>` or `!=` Not equals[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-or--not-equals "Direct link to -or--not-equals")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`(value1) <> (value2)` - returns true if `value1` is not equal to `value2`.

`!=` is an alias of `<>`.

#### Arguments[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#arguments-5 "Direct link to Arguments")

*   `value1` is any data type.
*   `value2` is any data type.

#### Return value[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#return-value-5 "Direct link to Return value")

Return value type is boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#examples-5 "Direct link to Examples")

    SELECT '5' <> '5';-- Returns falseSELECT 5 <> 5;-- Returns falseSELECT 'a' <> 'b';-- Returns trueSELECT 5 <> 3;-- Returns true

`IN` (value1, value2, ...)[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#in-value1-value2- "Direct link to in-value1-value2-")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The `IN` operator, when used with more than one argument, behaves as the standard SQL `IN`. It provides a concise way to represent multiple OR-ed equality conditions.

#### Arguments[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#arguments-6 "Direct link to Arguments")

*   `value1`, `value2`, ... are string type values representing dates or timestamps.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#examples-6 "Direct link to Examples")

Consider the following query:

IN list

    SELECT * FROM scoresWHERE ts IN ('2018-01-01', '2018-01-01T12:00', '2018-01-02');

This query is equivalent to:

IN list equivalent OR

    SELECT * FROM scoresWHERE ts = '2018-01-01' or ts = '2018-01-01T12:00' or ts = '2018-01-02';

| ts  | value |
| --- | --- |
| 2018-01-01T00:00:00.000000Z | 123.4 |
| 2018-01-01T12:00:00.000000Z | 589.1 |
| 2018-01-02T00:00:00.000000Z | 131.5 |

*   [`IN` (list)](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#in-list)
    
*   [`=` Equals](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-equals)
    
*   [`>` Greater than](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-greater-than)
    
*   [`>=` Greater than or equal to](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-greater-than-or-equal-to)
    
*   [`<` Lesser than](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-lesser-than)
    
*   [`<=` Lesser than or equal to](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-lesser-than-or-equal-to)
    
*   [`<>` or `!=` Not equals](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-or--not-equals)
    
*   [`IN` (value1, value2, ...)](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#in-value1-value2-)

---

# Logical Operators | QuestDB

On this page

`OR` Logical OR[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/logical/#or-logical-or "Direct link to or-logical-or")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`OR` represents a logical OR operation, which takes two predicates and filters for either one being true.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/logical/#examples "Direct link to Examples")

    SELECT * FROM (SELECT 5 AS a, 10 AS b) WHERE A = 5 OR B = 2

| a   | b   |
| --- | --- |
| 5   | 10  |

    SELECT * FROM (SELECT 5 AS a, 10 AS b) WHERE A = 3 OR B = 2

| a   | b   |
| --- | --- |

`AND` Logical AND[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/logical/#and-logical-and "Direct link to and-logical-and")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`AND` represents a logical AND operation, which takes two predicates and filters for both being true.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/logical/#examples-1 "Direct link to Examples")

    SELECT * FROM (SELECT 5 AS a, 10 AS b) WHERE A = 5 AND B = 2

| a   | b   |
| --- | --- |

    SELECT * FROM (SELECT 5 AS a, 10 AS b) WHERE A = 5 AND B = 10

| a   | b   |
| --- | --- |
| 5   | 10  |

`NOT` Logical NOT[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/logical/#not-logical-not "Direct link to not-logical-not")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`NOT` inverts the boolean value. This can be combined with other operators to create their inverse operations, i.e `NOT IN`, `NOT WITHIN`.

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/logical/#example "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT NOT TRUE

| column |
| --- |
| false |

*   [`OR` Logical OR](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/logical/#or-logical-or)
    
*   [`AND` Logical AND](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/logical/#and-logical-and)
    
*   [`NOT` Logical NOT](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/logical/#not-logical-not)

---

# SQL execution order | QuestDB

QuestDB attempts to implement standard ANSI SQL. We also try to be compatible with PostgreSQL, although parts of this are a work in progress. QuestDB implements these clauses which have the following execution order:

1.  [FROM](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/select/)
    
2.  [ON](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/join/)
    
3.  [JOIN](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/join/)
    
4.  [WHERE](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/where/)
    
5.  [LATEST ON](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/latest-on/)
    
6.  [GROUP BY](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/group-by/)
     (optional)
7.  [WITH](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/with/)
    
8.  [HAVING](https://questdb.com/docs/concepts/deep-dive/sql-extensions/#implicit-having)
     (implicit)
9.  [SELECT](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/select/)
    
10.  [DISTINCT](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/distinct/)
    
11.  [ORDER BY](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/order-by/)
    
12.  [LIMIT](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/limit/)
    

We have also implemented sub-queries that users may execute at any part of a query that mentions a table name. The sub-query implementation adds almost zero execution cost to SQL. We encourage the use of sub-queries as they add flavors of functional language features to traditional SQL.

For more information on the SQL extensions in QuestDB which deviate from ANSI SQL and PostgreSQL, see the [SQL extensions documentation](https://questdb.com/docs/concepts/deep-dive/sql-extensions/)
.

---

# Text Operators | QuestDB

On this page

`||` Concat[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#-concat "Direct link to -concat")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`||` concatenates strings, similar to [concat()](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/text/#concat)
.

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#example "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT 'a' || 'b'

| concat |
| --- |
| ab  |

`~` Regex match[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#-regex-match "Direct link to -regex-match")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Performs a regular-expression match on a string.

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#example-1 "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT address FROM (SELECT 'abc@foo.com' as address) WHERE address ~ '@foo.com'

| address |
| --- |
| [abc@foo.com](mailto:abc@foo.com) |

`!~` Regex doesn't match[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#-regex-doesnt-match "Direct link to -regex-doesnt-match")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The inverse of the `~` regex matching operator.

    SELECT address FROM (SELECT 'abc@foo.com' as address) WHERE address !~ '@bah.com'

| address |
| --- |
| [abc@foo.com](mailto:abc@foo.com) |

`LIKE`[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#like "Direct link to like")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`LIKE` performs a case-sensitive match, based on a pattern.

The `%` wildcard represents 0, 1 or n characters.

The `_` wildcard represents a single character.

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#example-2 "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT 'abc' LIKE '%c', 'abc' LIKE 'a_c'

| column | column1 |
| --- | --- |
| true | true |

`ILIKE`[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#ilike "Direct link to ilike")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`ILIKE` is the same as `LIKE`, but performs a case insensitive match,

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#example-3 "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT 'abC' LIKE '%c', 'abC' ILIKE '%c'

| column | column1 |
| --- | --- |
| false | true |

*   [`||` Concat](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#-concat)
    
*   [`~` Regex match](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#-regex-match)
    
*   [`!~` Regex doesn't match](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#-regex-doesnt-match)
    
*   [`LIKE`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#like)
    
*   [`ILIKE`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#ilike)

---

# Operator Precedence Table | QuestDB

On this page

The following tables provide information about which operators are available, and their corresponding precedences.

For IPv4 operators, this list is not comprehensive, and users should refer directly to the [IPv4](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/)
 documentation itself.

Pre-8.0 notice[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/precedence/#pre-80-notice "Direct link to Pre-8.0 notice")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In QuestDB 8.0.0, operator precedence is aligned closer to other SQL implementations.

If upgrading from 8.0, review your queries for any relevant changes.

If you are unable to migrate straight away, set the `cairo.sql.legacy.operator.precedence` config option to `true` in `server.conf`.

This is a temporary flag which will be removed in succeeding versions of QuestDB.

Legacy precedence, if set, is:

1.  `.`, `::`
2.  (none)
3.  `*`, `/`, `%`, `+`, `-`
4.  `<<`, `>>`, `<<=`, `>>=`
5.  `||`
6.  `<`, `>`, `<=`, \`>=
7.  `=`, `~`, `!=`, `<>`, `!~`, `IN`, `BETWEEN`, `LIKE`, `ILIKE`, `WITHIN`
8.  `&`
9.  `^`
10.  `|`
11.  `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`

See the next section for the current precedence.

### Current[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/precedence/#current "Direct link to Current")

| operator | name | precedence | description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [`.`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/misc/#-prefix) | prefix | 1   | prefix field with table name |
| [`::`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/misc/#-cast) | cast | 2   | postgres style type casting |
| [`-`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#--negate) | negate | 3   | unary negation of a number |
| [`~`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#-not) | complement | 3   | unary complement of a number |
| [`*`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#-multiply) | multiply | 4   | multiply two numbers |
| [`/`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#-divide) | divide | 4   | divide two numbers |
| [`%`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#-modulo) | modulo | 4   | take the modulo of two numbers |
| [`+`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#-add) | add | 5   | add two numbers |
| [`-`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#--subtract) | subtract | 5   | subtract two numbers |
| [`<<`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-left-strict-ip-address-contained-by) | left IPv4 contains strict | 6   |     |
| [`>>`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-right-strict-ip-address-contained-by) | right IPv4 contains strict | 6   |     |
| [`<<=`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-left-ip-address-contained-by-or-equal) | left IPv4 contains or equal | 6   |     |
| [`<<=`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-right-ip-address-contained-by-or-equal) | right IPv4 contains or equal | 6   |     |
| [`\|`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#-concat) | concat | 7   | concatenate strings |
| [`&`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#-and) | bitwise and | 8   | bitwise AND of two numbers |
| [`^`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#-xor) | bitwise xor | 9   | bitwise XOR of two numbers |
| [`\|`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#-or) | bitwise or | 10  | bitwise OR of two numbers |
| [`IN`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/date-time/#in-with-timestamp-intervals) | in  | 11  | check if value in list or range |
| [`BETWEEN`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/date-time/#between--and) | between | 11  | check if timestamp in range |
| [`WITHIN`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/spatial/#within) | within geohash | 11  | prefix matches geohash |
| [`<`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-lesser-than) | lesser than | 12  | lt comparison |
| [`<=`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-lesser-than-or-equal-to) | lesser than or equal to | 12  | leq comparison |
| [`>`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-greater-than) | greater than | 12  | gt comparison |
| [`>=`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-greater-than-or-equal-to) | greater than or equal to | 12  | geq comparison |
| [`=`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-equals) | equals | 13  | eq comparison |
| [`~`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#-regex-match) | regex match | 13  | regex pattern match |
| [`!=`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-or--not-equals) | not equals | 13  | neq comparison |
| [`<>`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/comparison/#-or--not-equals) | not equals | 13  | neq comparison |
| [`!~`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#-regex-doesnt-match) | regex does not match | 13  | regex pattern does not match |
| [`LIKE`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#like) | match string | 13  | pattern matching |
| [`ILIKE`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/text/#ilike) | match string without case | 13  | case insensitive pattern matching |
| [`NOT`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/logical/#not-logical-not) | logical not | 14  | logical NOT of two numbers |
| [`AND`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/logical/#and-logical-and) | logical and | 15  | logical AND of two numbers |
| [`OR`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/logical/#or-logical-or) | logical or | 16  | logical OR of two numbers |

*   [Pre-8.0 notice](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/precedence/#pre-80-notice)
    *   [Current](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/precedence/#current)
