# MACD (Moving Average Convergence Divergence) | QuestDB

On this page

MACD (Moving Average Convergence Divergence) is a trend-following momentum indicator that shows the relationship between two exponential moving averages. It consists of the MACD line, signal line, and histogram.

Problem[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/macd/#problem "Direct link to Problem")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You want to identify trend changes and momentum shifts. Simple moving averages lag too much, while raw price changes are too noisy. MACD combines fast and slow EMAs to filter noise while remaining responsive to trend changes.

Solution[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/macd/#solution "Direct link to Solution")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Calculate MACD with signal line and histogram[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=DECLARE%0A%20%20%40symbol%20%3A%3D%20%27EURUSD%27%2C%0A%20%20%40lookback%20%3A%3D%20%27%24now%20-%202d..%24now%27%0A%0AWITH%20ema%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20avg(close%2C%20%27period%27%2C%2012)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20ema12%2C%0A%20%20%20%20avg(close%2C%20%27period%27%2C%2026)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20ema26%0A%20%20FROM%20market_data_ohlc_15m%0A%20%20WHERE%20symbol%20%3D%20%40symbol%0A%20%20%20%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%40lookback%0A%20%20WINDOW%20w%20AS%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp)%0A)%2C%0Amacd_line%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20ema12%2C%0A%20%20%20%20ema26%2C%0A%20%20%20%20ema12%20-%20ema26%20AS%20macd%0A%20%20FROM%20ema%0A)%2C%0Awith_signal%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20macd%2C%0A%20%20%20%20avg(macd%2C%20%27period%27%2C%209)%20OVER%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp)%20AS%20signal%0A%20%20FROM%20macd_line%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20round(close%2C%205)%20AS%20close%2C%0A%20%20round(macd%2C%206)%20AS%20macd%2C%0A%20%20round(signal%2C%206)%20AS%20signal%2C%0A%20%20round(macd%20-%20signal%2C%206)%20AS%20histogram%0AFROM%20with_signal%0AORDER%20BY%20timestamp%3B&executeQuery=true)

    DECLARE  @symbol := 'EURUSD',  @lookback := '$now - 2d..$now'WITH ema AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    close,    avg(close, 'period', 12) OVER w AS ema12,    avg(close, 'period', 26) OVER w AS ema26  FROM market_data_ohlc_15m  WHERE symbol = @symbol    AND timestamp IN @lookback  WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp)),macd_line AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    close,    ema12,    ema26,    ema12 - ema26 AS macd  FROM ema),with_signal AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    close,    macd,    avg(macd, 'period', 9) OVER (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp) AS signal  FROM macd_line)SELECT  timestamp,  symbol,  round(close, 5) AS close,  round(macd, 6) AS macd,  round(signal, 6) AS signal,  round(macd - signal, 6) AS histogramFROM with_signalORDER BY timestamp;

The query:

1.  Calculates 12-period and 26-period EMAs using `avg(value, 'period', N)`
2.  Computes MACD line as the difference: EMA12 - EMA26
3.  Calculates 9-period EMA of MACD as the signal line
4.  Derives histogram as MACD - signal

Interpreting results[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/macd/#interpreting-results "Direct link to Interpreting results")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **MACD crosses above signal**: Bullish signal, momentum turning up
*   **MACD crosses below signal**: Bearish signal, momentum turning down
*   **Histogram growing**: Momentum strengthening in current direction
*   **Histogram shrinking**: Momentum weakening, potential reversal
*   **MACD above zero**: Uptrend (fast EMA above slow EMA)
*   **MACD below zero**: Downtrend (fast EMA below slow EMA)

Standard parameters

The classic MACD uses 12/26/9 periods. Some traders adjust these:

*   Faster signals: 8/17/9
*   Slower signals: 19/39/9

Related documentation

*   [EMA window function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/reference/#avg)
    
*   [Window functions overview](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/overview/)
    

*   [Problem](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/macd/#problem)
    
*   [Solution](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/macd/#solution)
    
*   [Interpreting results](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/macd/#interpreting-results)

---

# OBV (On-Balance Volume) | QuestDB

On this page

On-Balance Volume (OBV) is a cumulative indicator that adds volume on up days and subtracts volume on down days. It shows whether volume is flowing into or out of an asset, often leading price movements.

Problem[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/obv/#problem "Direct link to Problem")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You want to confirm price trends with volume or spot divergences where volume doesn't support price movement. Raw volume numbers don't show direction, and comparing volumes across different time periods is difficult.

Solution[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/obv/#solution "Direct link to Solution")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Calculate On-Balance Volume[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=DECLARE%0A%20%20%40symbol%20%3A%3D%20%27EURUSD%27%2C%0A%20%20%40lookback%20%3A%3D%20%27%24now%20-%201M..%24now%27%0A%0AWITH%20ohlc%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20last(price)%20AS%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20sum(quantity)%20AS%20volume%0A%20%20FROM%20fx_trades%0A%20%20WHERE%20symbol%20%3D%20%40symbol%0A%20%20%20%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%40lookback%0A%20%20SAMPLE%20BY%2015m%20ALIGN%20TO%20CALENDAR%0A)%2C%0Awith_direction%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20volume%2C%0A%20%20%20%20CASE%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20WHEN%20close%20%3E%20lag(close)%20OVER%20w%20THEN%20volume%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20WHEN%20close%20%3C%20lag(close)%20OVER%20w%20THEN%20-volume%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20ELSE%200%0A%20%20%20%20END%20AS%20directed_volume%0A%20%20FROM%20ohlc%0A%20%20WINDOW%20w%20AS%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp)%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20round(close%2C%205)%20AS%20close%2C%0A%20%20round(volume%2C%200)%20AS%20volume%2C%0A%20%20round(sum(directed_volume)%20OVER%20(%0A%20%20%20%20PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%0A%20%20%20%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp%0A%20%20%20%20ROWS%20BETWEEN%20UNBOUNDED%20PRECEDING%20AND%20CURRENT%20ROW%0A%20%20)%2C%200)%20AS%20obv%0AFROM%20with_direction%0AORDER%20BY%20timestamp%3B&executeQuery=true)

    DECLARE  @symbol := 'EURUSD',  @lookback := '$now - 1M..$now'WITH ohlc AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    last(price) AS close,    sum(quantity) AS volume  FROM fx_trades  WHERE symbol = @symbol    AND timestamp IN @lookback  SAMPLE BY 15m ALIGN TO CALENDAR),with_direction AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    close,    volume,    CASE      WHEN close > lag(close) OVER w THEN volume      WHEN close < lag(close) OVER w THEN -volume      ELSE 0    END AS directed_volume  FROM ohlc  WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp))SELECT  timestamp,  symbol,  round(close, 5) AS close,  round(volume, 0) AS volume,  round(sum(directed_volume) OVER (    PARTITION BY symbol    ORDER BY timestamp    ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW  ), 0) AS obvFROM with_directionORDER BY timestamp;

The query:

1.  Aggregates raw trades into 15-minute bars with closing price and total volume
2.  Compares each bar's close to the previous close
3.  Assigns positive volume if price went up, negative if down, zero if unchanged
4.  Calculates cumulative sum of directed volume

Interpreting results[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/obv/#interpreting-results "Direct link to Interpreting results")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **OBV rising with price**: Uptrend confirmed by volume
*   **OBV falling with price**: Downtrend confirmed by volume
*   **OBV rising, price flat**: Accumulation, potential breakout up
*   **OBV falling, price flat**: Distribution, potential breakout down
*   **OBV divergence from price**: Trend may be weakening

OBV absolute value

The absolute value of OBV is meaningless. What matters is the direction and whether it confirms or diverges from price.

Related documentation

*   [sum() window function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/reference/#sum)
    
*   [lag() function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/reference/#lag)
    

*   [Problem](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/obv/#problem)
    
*   [Solution](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/obv/#solution)
    
*   [Interpreting results](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/obv/#interpreting-results)

---

# Numeric Operators | QuestDB

On this page

These operations work for any numeric types. Also, addition and multiplication work for N-dimensional arrays. The result will be an array where each element is the result of applying the operation to the elements at the same coordinates in the operand arrays.

`*` Multiply[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#-multiply "Direct link to -multiply")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`*` is a binary operation to multiply two numbers together.

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#example "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT 5 + 2

| column |
| --- |
| 7   |

`/` Divide[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#-divide "Direct link to -divide")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`/` is a binary operation to divide two numbers.

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#example-1 "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT 5 / 2, 5.0 / 2.0

| column | column1 |
| --- | --- |
| 2   | 2.5 |

`%` Modulo[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#-modulo "Direct link to -modulo")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`%` performs a modulo operation, returning the remainder of a division.

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#example-2 "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT 5 % 2

| column |
| --- |
| 1   |

`+` Add[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#-add "Direct link to -add")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`+` performs an addition operation, for two numbers.

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#example-3 "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT 5 + 2

| column |
| --- |
| 7   |

`-` Subtract[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#--subtract "Direct link to --subtract")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`-` performs a subtraction operation, for two numbers.

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#example-4 "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT 5 - 2

| column |
| --- |
| 3   |

`-` Negate[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#--negate "Direct link to --negate")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`-` can also be used for unary negation.

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#example-5 "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT -5

| column |
| --- |
| \-5 |

*   [`*` Multiply](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#-multiply)
    
*   [`/` Divide](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#-divide)
    
*   [`%` Modulo](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#-modulo)
    
*   [`+` Add](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#-add)
    
*   [`-` Subtract](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#--subtract)
    
*   [`-` Negate](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/numeric/#--negate)

---

# IPv4 Operators | QuestDB

On this page

This document outlines the IPv4 data type operators.

The IP addresses can be in the range of `0.0.0.1` - `255.255.255.255`.

The address: `0.0.0.0` is interpreted as `NULL`.

The following operators support `string` type arguments to permit the passing of netmasks:

*   `<<` [Strict IP address contained by](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-left-strict-ip-address-contained-by)
    
*   `<<=` [IP address contained by or equal](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-left-ip-address-contained-by-or-equal)
    
*   [rnd\_ipv4()](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/random-value-generator/#rnd_ipv4)
    
*   [netmask()](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#return-netmask---netmaskstring)
    

`<` Less than[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-less-than "Direct link to -less-than")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes two IPv4 arguments.

Returns a boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: testing to see if one IP address is less than another.

    ipv4 '33.1.8.43' < ipv4 '200.6.38.9' -> T

`<=` Less than or equal[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-less-than-or-equal "Direct link to -less-than-or-equal")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes two IPv4 arguments.

Returns a boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-1 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: testing to see if one IP address is less than or equal to another.

    ipv4 '33.1.8.43' <= ipv4 '33.1.8.43' -> T

`>` Greater than[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-greater-than "Direct link to -greater-than")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes two IPv4 arguments.

Returns a boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-2 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: testing to see if one IP address is greater than another.

    ipv4 '33.1.8.43' > ipv4 '200.6.38.9' -> F

`>=` Greater than or equal[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-greater-than-or-equal "Direct link to -greater-than-or-equal")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes two IPv4 arguments.

Returns a boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-3 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: testing to see if one IP address is greater than or equal to another.

    ipv4 '33.1.8.43' >= ipv4 '200.6.38.9' -> F

`=` Equals[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-equals "Direct link to -equals")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes two IPv4 arguments.

Returns a boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-4 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: testing to see if one IP address is equal to another.

    ipv4 '44.8.9.10' = ipv4 '6.2.90.1' -> F

`!=` Does not equal[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-does-not-equal "Direct link to -does-not-equal")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes two IPv4 arguments.

Returns a boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-5 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: testing to see if one IP address is not equal to another.

    ipv4 '44.8.9.10' != ipv4 '6.2.90.1' -> T

`<<` Left strict IP address contained by[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-left-strict-ip-address-contained-by "Direct link to -left-strict-ip-address-contained-by")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes one IPv4 argument and one string argument.

The string argument can accept IPv4 addresses with a subnet mask, the IPv4 argument cannot.

Returns a boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-6 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: searching ip addresses by subnet

    ipv4 '35.24.65.11' << '35.24.65.2/16' -> Tipv4 '35.24.65.11' << '35.24.65.2/32' -> F

`>>` Right strict IP address contained by[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-right-strict-ip-address-contained-by "Direct link to -right-strict-ip-address-contained-by")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes one IPv4 argument and one string argument.

The string argument can accept IPv4 addresses with a subnet mask, the IPv4 argument cannot.

Returns a boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-7 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: searching ip addresses by subnet

    '35.24.65.2/16' >> ipv4 '35.24.65.11' -> T'35.24.65.2/32'  >> ipv4 '35.24.65.11' -> F

`<<=` Left IP address contained by or equal[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-left-ip-address-contained-by-or-equal "Direct link to -left-ip-address-contained-by-or-equal")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes one IPv4 argument and one string argument

The string argument can accept IPv4 addresses with a subnet mask, the IPv4 argument cannot.

Returns a boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-8 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: searching ip addresses by subnet

    ipv4 '35.24.65.11' <<= '35.24.65.2/16' -> Tipv4 '35.24.65.11' <<= '35.24.65.2/32' -> T

`<<=` Right IP address contained by or equal[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-right-ip-address-contained-by-or-equal "Direct link to -right-ip-address-contained-by-or-equal")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes one IPv4 argument and one string argument

The string argument can accept IPv4 addresses with a subnet mask, the IPv4 argument cannot.

Returns a boolean.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-9 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: searching ip addresses by subnet

    '35.24.65.2/16' >>= ipv4 '35.24.65.11'  -> T'35.24.65.2/32' >>= ipv4 '35.24.65.11'  -> T

`&` Bitwise AND[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-bitwise-and "Direct link to -bitwise-and")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes two IPv4 arguments.

Returns an IPv4 address.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-10 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: separating an ip address into its network and host portions

    ipv4 '215.53.40.9' & ipv4 '255.255.0.0' -> 215.53.0.0ipv4 '99.8.63.41' & ipv4 '0.0.63.41' -> 0.0.63.41

`~` Bitwise NOT[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-bitwise-not "Direct link to -bitwise-not")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes one IPv4 argument.

Returns an IPv4 address.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-11 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: computing broadcast address' bitmask from a netmask

    ~ ipv4 '255.255.0.0' -> 0.0.255.255

`|` Bitwise OR[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-bitwise-or "Direct link to -bitwise-or")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes two IPv4 arguments.

Returns an IPv4 address.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-12 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: computing an ip address' broadcast address

    ipv4 '92.11.8.40' | '0.0.255.255' -> 92.11.255.255

`+` Add offset to an IP address[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-add-offset-to-an-ip-address "Direct link to -add-offset-to-an-ip-address")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes one IPv4 argument and one integer argument.

Returns an IPv4 address.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-13 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: altering an ip address

    ipv4 '92.11.8.40' + 5 -> 92.11.8.4510 + ipv4 '2.6.43.8' -> 2.6.43.18

`-` Subtract offset from IP address[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#--subtract-offset-from-ip-address "Direct link to --subtract-offset-from-ip-address")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes one IPv4 argument and one integer argument.

Returns an IPv4 address.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-14 "Direct link to Examples")

    ipv4 '92.11.8.40' - 5 -> 92.11.8.35

`-` Difference between two IP addresses[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#--difference-between-two-ip-addresses "Direct link to --difference-between-two-ip-addresses")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes two IPv4 arguments.

Returns a long.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-15 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: calculating the range of unique addresses between two ip addresses

    ipv4 '92.11.8.40' - ipv4 '92.11.8.0' -> 40

Return netmask - netmask(string)[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#return-netmask---netmaskstring "Direct link to Return netmask - netmask(string)")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Takes a `string` IPv4 argument as either:

*   ipv4 address with a netmask `22.59.138.9/8`
*   subnet with netmask: `2.2/16`

Returns an IPv4 addresses' netmask (`255.0.0.0`) in IPv4 format.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#examples-16 "Direct link to Examples")

Use case: Obtaining the broadcast bitmask for an ip address via performing bitwise NOT on the netmask.

Apply a bitwise OR to this result to obtain the broadcast address of an ip address.

    ~ netmask('68.11.9.2/8')) | ipv4 '68.11.9.2' -> 68.255.255.255

*   [`<` Less than](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-less-than)
    
*   [`<=` Less than or equal](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-less-than-or-equal)
    
*   [`>` Greater than](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-greater-than)
    
*   [`>=` Greater than or equal](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-greater-than-or-equal)
    
*   [`=` Equals](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-equals)
    
*   [`!=` Does not equal](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-does-not-equal)
    
*   [`<<` Left strict IP address contained by](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-left-strict-ip-address-contained-by)
    
*   [`>>` Right strict IP address contained by](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-right-strict-ip-address-contained-by)
    
*   [`<<=` Left IP address contained by or equal](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-left-ip-address-contained-by-or-equal)
    
*   [`<<=` Right IP address contained by or equal](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-right-ip-address-contained-by-or-equal)
    
*   [`&` Bitwise AND](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-bitwise-and)
    
*   [`~` Bitwise NOT](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-bitwise-not)
    
*   [`|` Bitwise OR](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-bitwise-or)
    
*   [`+` Add offset to an IP address](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#-add-offset-to-an-ip-address)
    
*   [`-` Subtract offset from IP address](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#--subtract-offset-from-ip-address)
    
*   [`-` Difference between two IP addresses](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#--difference-between-two-ip-addresses)
    
*   [Return netmask - netmask(string)](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/ipv4/#return-netmask---netmaskstring)

# Bitwise Operators | QuestDB

On this page

This page describes the available operators to assist with performing bitwise operations on numeric values.

`~` NOT[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#-not "Direct link to -not")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`~` is a unary operation that performs bitwise negation on each bit. Bits that are 0 become 1, and those that are 1 become 0. Expects a value of `long` or `int` type.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#examples "Direct link to Examples")

    SELECT ~1024

| column |
| --- |
| \-1025 |

`&` AND[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#-and "Direct link to -and")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`&` is a binary operation that takes two equal-length binary representations and performs the bitwise AND operation on each pair of the corresponding bits. Expects values of `long` or `int` type.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#examples-1 "Direct link to Examples")

    SELECT 5 & 3

| column |
| --- |
| 1   |

`^` XOR[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#-xor "Direct link to -xor")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`^` is a binary operation that takes two bit patterns of equal length and performs the bitwise exclusive OR (XOR) operation on each pair of corresponding bits. Expects a value of `long` or `int` type.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#examples-2 "Direct link to Examples")

    SELECT 5 ^ 3

| column |
| --- |
| 6   |

`|` OR[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#-or "Direct link to -or")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`|` is a binary operation that takes two bit patterns of equal length and performs the logical inclusive OR operation on each pair of corresponding bits. Expects a value of `long` or `int` type.

#### Examples[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#examples-3 "Direct link to Examples")

    SELECT 5 | 3

| column |
| --- |
| 7   |

*   [`~` NOT](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#-not)
    
*   [`&` AND](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#-and)
    
*   [`^` XOR](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#-xor)
    
*   [`|` OR](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/bitwise/#-or)

---

# Log returns | QuestDB

On this page

Log returns measure the relative change between consecutive prices using the natural logarithm. They are preferred over simple returns in financial analysis because they are additive over time and symmetric.

Problem[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/log-returns/#problem "Direct link to Problem")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You want to compute log returns between consecutive price observations.

Solution[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/log-returns/#solution "Direct link to Solution")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Use `LAG()` to access the previous row's value, then compute the natural log of the price ratio.

### Tick-by-tick log returns[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/log-returns/#tick-by-tick-log-returns "Direct link to Tick-by-tick log returns")

This example computes log returns from the mid-price of individual quotes:

Log returns from mid-price[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=DECLARE%0A%20%20%40symbol%20%3A%3D%20%27EURUSD%27%2C%0A%20%20%40lookback%20%3A%3D%20%27%24now%20-%201m..%24now%27%0A%0ASELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20round((bid_price%20%2B%20ask_price)%20%2F%202%2C%205)%20AS%20mid_price%2C%0A%20%20LN(%0A%20%20%20%20(bid_price%20%2B%20ask_price)%20%2F%202%20%2F%0A%20%20%20%20LAG((bid_price%20%2B%20ask_price)%20%2F%202)%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20OVER%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp)%0A%20%20)%20AS%20log_return%0AFROM%20core_price%0AWHERE%20symbol%20%3D%20%40symbol%0A%20%20AND%20ecn%20%3D%20%27LMAX%27%0A%20%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%40lookback%3B&executeQuery=true)

    DECLARE  @symbol := 'EURUSD',  @lookback := '$now - 1m..$now'SELECT  timestamp,  symbol,  round((bid_price + ask_price) / 2, 5) AS mid_price,  LN(    (bid_price + ask_price) / 2 /    LAG((bid_price + ask_price) / 2)        OVER (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp)  ) AS log_returnFROM core_priceWHERE symbol = @symbol  AND ecn = 'LMAX'  AND timestamp IN @lookback;

The `core_price` table contains quotes from multiple ECNs (LMAX, EBS, Currenex, Hotspot). Filtering to a single ECN avoids mixing venue-level price differences into returns - without the filter, consecutive rows may come from different venues, producing spurious returns that reflect venue spreads rather than true price moves.

Tick returns and microstructure noise

Raw tick-level log returns overstate volatility due to bid-ask bounce: even if the true price is unchanged, alternating bid- and ask-side quotes produce non-zero returns. For volatility estimation, use the [fixed-frequency](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/log-returns/#fixed-frequency-log-returns)
 approach below, which aggregates ticks into bars before computing returns.

### Fixed-frequency log returns[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/log-returns/#fixed-frequency-log-returns "Direct link to Fixed-frequency log returns")

For returns at a fixed frequency, sample first then compute returns on the sampled result:

One-minute log returns[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=DECLARE%0A%20%20%40symbol%20%3A%3D%20%27EURUSD%27%2C%0A%20%20%40lookback%20%3A%3D%20%27%24now%20-%201d..%24now%27%0A%0AWITH%20sampled%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20last((bid_price%20%2B%20ask_price)%20%2F%202)%20AS%20close_mid%0A%20%20FROM%20core_price%0A%20%20WHERE%20symbol%20%3D%20%40symbol%0A%20%20%20%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%40lookback%0A%20%20SAMPLE%20BY%201m%20ALIGN%20TO%20CALENDAR%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20round(close_mid%2C%205)%20AS%20close_mid%2C%0A%20%20LN(close_mid%20%2F%20LAG(close_mid)%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20OVER%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp))%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20AS%20log_return%0AFROM%20sampled%3B&executeQuery=true)

    DECLARE  @symbol := 'EURUSD',  @lookback := '$now - 1d..$now'WITH sampled AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    last((bid_price + ask_price) / 2) AS close_mid  FROM core_price  WHERE symbol = @symbol    AND timestamp IN @lookback  SAMPLE BY 1m ALIGN TO CALENDAR)SELECT  timestamp,  symbol,  round(close_mid, 5) AS close_mid,  LN(close_mid / LAG(close_mid)      OVER (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp))      AS log_returnFROM sampled;

How it works[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/log-returns/#how-it-works "Direct link to How it works")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Log returns are defined as `ln(P_t / P_{t-1})` where `P_t` is the current price and `P_{t-1}` is the previous price. They are preferred over simple returns because:

*   **Additive over time**: you can sum log returns across periods to get the total return
*   **Symmetric**: a round-trip always sums to zero. If the price goes from 100 to 110 and back to 100, the log returns are `ln(110/100) + ln(100/110) = 0`. With simple returns the same round-trip gives asymmetric percentages (+10% up, -9.09% down)

`LAG(value) OVER (ORDER BY timestamp)` gives the previous row's value in timestamp order. The first row returns `NULL` since there is no predecessor, which propagates through `LN` as `NULL`.

When computing returns at a fixed frequency, always aggregate into bars first with `SAMPLE BY` in a CTE, then apply `LAG()` to the sampled result. This ensures the window function runs over the small number of bars (e.g. 1,440 for one-minute bars over a day) rather than millions of raw ticks. See [this blog post](https://questdb.com/blog/sample-by-window-function-order/)
 for a detailed walkthrough of why the order matters.

Related documentation

*   [Realized Volatility](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/realized-volatility/)
     - uses log returns to compute rolling volatility
*   [Cumulative Product](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/cumulative-product/)
     - running product of returns
*   [Rolling Std Dev](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/rolling-stddev/)
     - rolling standard deviation of prices
*   [Window functions](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/overview/)
    
*   [ln() function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/numeric/#ln)
    

*   [Problem](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/log-returns/#problem)
    
*   [Solution](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/log-returns/#solution)
    *   [Tick-by-tick log returns](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/log-returns/#tick-by-tick-log-returns)
        
    *   [Fixed-frequency log returns](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/log-returns/#fixed-frequency-log-returns)
        
*   [How it works](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/log-returns/#how-it-works)

---

# Rate of Change (ROC) | QuestDB

On this page

Rate of Change (ROC) measures the percentage change in price between the current price and the price N periods ago. It oscillates around zero, with positive values indicating upward momentum and negative values indicating downward momentum.

Problem[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/rate-of-change/#problem "Direct link to Problem")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You want a simple momentum indicator that shows how fast price is changing. Raw price differences don't account for the price level, making comparison across assets difficult.

Solution[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/rate-of-change/#solution "Direct link to Solution")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Calculate 12-period Rate of Change[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=DECLARE%0A%20%20%40symbol%20%3A%3D%20%27EURUSD%27%2C%0A%20%20%40lookback%20%3A%3D%20%27%24now%20-%202d..%24now%27%0A%0AWITH%20with_lag%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20lag(close%2C%2012)%20OVER%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp)%20AS%20close_12_ago%0A%20%20FROM%20market_data_ohlc_15m%0A%20%20WHERE%20symbol%20%3D%20%40symbol%0A%20%20%20%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%40lookback%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20round(close%2C%205)%20AS%20close%2C%0A%20%20round(close_12_ago%2C%205)%20AS%20close_12_ago%2C%0A%20%20round((close%20-%20close_12_ago)%20%2F%20close_12_ago%20*%20100%2C%204)%20AS%20roc%0AFROM%20with_lag%0AWHERE%20close_12_ago%20IS%20NOT%20NULL%0AORDER%20BY%20timestamp%3B&executeQuery=true)

    DECLARE  @symbol := 'EURUSD',  @lookback := '$now - 2d..$now'WITH with_lag AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    close,    lag(close, 12) OVER (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp) AS close_12_ago  FROM market_data_ohlc_15m  WHERE symbol = @symbol    AND timestamp IN @lookback)SELECT  timestamp,  symbol,  round(close, 5) AS close,  round(close_12_ago, 5) AS close_12_ago,  round((close - close_12_ago) / close_12_ago * 100, 4) AS rocFROM with_lagWHERE close_12_ago IS NOT NULLORDER BY timestamp;

The formula: `ROC = ((Close - Close N periods ago) / Close N periods ago) × 100`

Interpreting results[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/rate-of-change/#interpreting-results "Direct link to Interpreting results")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **ROC > 0**: Price higher than N periods ago, upward momentum
*   **ROC < 0**: Price lower than N periods ago, downward momentum
*   **ROC crossing zero**: Potential trend change
*   **Extreme ROC values**: Overbought/oversold, may revert to mean

Period selection

Common ROC periods:

*   **9 or 12**: Short-term momentum
*   **25**: Medium-term (roughly one month of daily data)
*   **200**: Long-term trend

Related documentation

*   [lag() function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/reference/#lag)
    
*   [Window functions overview](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/overview/)
    

*   [Problem](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/rate-of-change/#problem)
    
*   [Solution](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/rate-of-change/#solution)
    
*   [Interpreting results](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/rate-of-change/#interpreting-results)

---

# TICK and TRIN indicators | QuestDB

On this page

Calculate TICK and TRIN (Trading Index, also known as the ARMS Index) to measure market breadth. These indicators count how many symbols are advancing versus declining across a market.

TICK[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#tick "Direct link to TICK")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**TICK** measures market direction by counting symbols:

*   For each symbol, check if its last trade price > previous trade price (uptick) or < (downtick)
*   TICK = number of symbols on uptick - number of symbols on downtick
*   Positive TICK = more symbols rising, negative = more falling

### TICK snapshot[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#tick-snapshot "Direct link to TICK snapshot")

Calculate a single market-wide TICK value. For each symbol, compare the last trade price to the previous trade price to determine if it's on an uptick or downtick. The final result is one row showing how many symbols are rising versus falling.

TICK - market breadth snapshot[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=WITH%20with_previous%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%20timestamp%2C%20symbol%2C%20price%2C%0A%20%20%20%20LAG(price)%20OVER%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp)%20AS%20prev_price%0A%20%20FROM%20fx_trades%0A%20%20WHERE%20timestamp%20IN%20%27%24today%27%0A)%2C%0Aclassified%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20CASE%20WHEN%20price%20%3E%20prev_price%20THEN%201%20ELSE%200%20END%20AS%20is_uptick%2C%0A%20%20%20%20CASE%20WHEN%20price%20%3C%20prev_price%20THEN%201%20ELSE%200%20END%20AS%20is_downtick%0A%20%20FROM%20with_previous%0A%20%20WHERE%20prev_price%20IS%20NOT%20NULL%0A%20%20LATEST%20ON%20timestamp%20PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20--%20use%20only%20the%20latest%20entry%20per%20symbol%2C%20together%20with%20the%20previous%20price%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20SUM(is_uptick)%20AS%20uptick_symbols%2C%0A%20%20SUM(is_downtick)%20AS%20downtick_symbols%2C%0A%20%20SUM(is_uptick)%20-%20SUM(is_downtick)%20AS%20tick%0AFROM%20classified%3B&executeQuery=true)

    WITH with_previous AS (  SELECT timestamp, symbol, price,    LAG(price) OVER (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp) AS prev_price  FROM fx_trades  WHERE timestamp IN '$today'),classified AS (  SELECT  symbol,    CASE WHEN price > prev_price THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_uptick,    CASE WHEN price < prev_price THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_downtick  FROM with_previous  WHERE prev_price IS NOT NULL  LATEST ON timestamp PARTITION BY symbol -- use only the latest entry per symbol, together with the previous price)SELECT  SUM(is_uptick) AS uptick_symbols,  SUM(is_downtick) AS downtick_symbols,  SUM(is_uptick) - SUM(is_downtick) AS tickFROM classified;

### Interpreting TICK[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#interpreting-tick "Direct link to Interpreting TICK")

*   **Positive**: More symbols on uptick (bullish)
*   **Negative**: More symbols on downtick (bearish)
*   **Near zero**: Balanced market

TRIN[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#trin "Direct link to TRIN")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**TRIN** (ARMS Index) adds volume weighting:

*   TRIN = (advancing symbols / declining symbols) / (advancing volume / declining volume)
*   TRIN < 1.0 = volume favoring advances (bullish)
*   TRIN > 1.0 = volume favoring declines (bearish)
*   TRIN = 1.0 = neutral

### TRIN snapshot[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#trin-snapshot "Direct link to TRIN snapshot")

Calculate a single market-wide TRIN value. For each symbol, aggregate intraday volume and classify it as advancing or declining based on whether the current price is above or below the day's open. The final result is one row showing overall market breadth.

TRIN - daily breadth snapshot[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=WITH%20daily_stats%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20first(price)%20AS%20open_price%2C%0A%20%20%20%20last(price)%20AS%20current_price%2C%0A%20%20%20%20sum(quantity)%20AS%20total_volume%0A%20%20FROM%20fx_trades%0A%20%20WHERE%20timestamp%20IN%20%27%24today%27%0A%20%20SAMPLE%20BY%201d%0A)%2C%0Aclassified%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%20*%2C%0A%20%20%20%20CASE%20WHEN%20current_price%20%3E%20open_price%20THEN%201%20ELSE%200%20END%20AS%20is_advancing%2C%0A%20%20%20%20CASE%20WHEN%20current_price%20%3C%20open_price%20THEN%201%20ELSE%200%20END%20AS%20is_declining%0A%20%20FROM%20daily_stats%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20SUM(is_advancing)%20AS%20advancing%2C%0A%20%20SUM(is_declining)%20AS%20declining%2C%0A%20%20SUM(CASE%20WHEN%20is_advancing%20%3D%201%20THEN%20total_volume%20ELSE%200%20END)%20AS%20advancing_volume%2C%0A%20%20SUM(CASE%20WHEN%20is_declining%20%3D%201%20THEN%20total_volume%20ELSE%200%20END)%20AS%20declining_volume%2C%0A%20%20(SUM(is_advancing)%3A%3Adouble%20%2F%20NULLIF(SUM(is_declining)%2C%200))%20%2F%0A%20%20(SUM(CASE%20WHEN%20is_advancing%20%3D%201%20THEN%20total_volume%20ELSE%200%20END)%3A%3Adouble%20%2F%0A%20%20%20NULLIF(SUM(CASE%20WHEN%20is_declining%20%3D%201%20THEN%20total_volume%20ELSE%200%20END)%2C%200))%20AS%20trin%0AFROM%20classified%3B&executeQuery=true)

    WITH daily_stats AS (  SELECT symbol,    first(price) AS open_price,    last(price) AS current_price,    sum(quantity) AS total_volume  FROM fx_trades  WHERE timestamp IN '$today'  SAMPLE BY 1d),classified AS (  SELECT *,    CASE WHEN current_price > open_price THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_advancing,    CASE WHEN current_price < open_price THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_declining  FROM daily_stats)SELECT  SUM(is_advancing) AS advancing,  SUM(is_declining) AS declining,  SUM(CASE WHEN is_advancing = 1 THEN total_volume ELSE 0 END) AS advancing_volume,  SUM(CASE WHEN is_declining = 1 THEN total_volume ELSE 0 END) AS declining_volume,  (SUM(is_advancing)::double / NULLIF(SUM(is_declining), 0)) /  (SUM(CASE WHEN is_advancing = 1 THEN total_volume ELSE 0 END)::double /   NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN is_declining = 1 THEN total_volume ELSE 0 END), 0)) AS trinFROM classified;

### TRIN time-series[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#trin-time-series "Direct link to TRIN time-series")

Track how market breadth evolves throughout the day. For each candle interval, compare each symbol's close to its previous close to classify it as advancing or declining. Each row returns the market-wide TRIN at that point in time.

TRIN time-series with 5-minute candles[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=WITH%20candles%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%20timestamp%2C%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20last(price)%20AS%20close_price%2C%0A%20%20%20%20sum(quantity)%20AS%20total_volume%0A%20%20FROM%20fx_trades%0A%20%20WHERE%20timestamp%20IN%20%27%24today%27%0A%20%20SAMPLE%20BY%205m%0A)%2C%0Awith_previous%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%20timestamp%2C%20symbol%2C%20total_volume%2C%20close_price%2C%0A%20%20%20%20LAG(close_price)%20OVER%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp)%20AS%20last_close%0A%20%20FROM%20candles%0A)%2C%0Aclassified%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%20timestamp%2C%20symbol%2C%20total_volume%2C%0A%20%20%20%20CASE%20WHEN%20close_price%20%3E%20last_close%20THEN%201%20ELSE%200%20END%20AS%20is_advancing%2C%0A%20%20%20%20CASE%20WHEN%20close_price%20%3C%20last_close%20THEN%201%20ELSE%200%20END%20AS%20is_declining%0A%20%20FROM%20with_previous%0A%20%20WHERE%20last_close%20IS%20NOT%20NULL%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20SUM(is_advancing)%20AS%20advancing%2C%0A%20%20SUM(is_declining)%20AS%20declining%2C%0A%20%20SUM(CASE%20WHEN%20is_advancing%20%3D%201%20THEN%20total_volume%20ELSE%200%20END)%20AS%20advancing_volume%2C%0A%20%20SUM(CASE%20WHEN%20is_declining%20%3D%201%20THEN%20total_volume%20ELSE%200%20END)%20AS%20declining_volume%2C%0A%20%20(SUM(is_advancing)%3A%3Adouble%20%2F%20NULLIF(SUM(is_declining)%2C%200))%20%2F%0A%20%20(SUM(CASE%20WHEN%20is_advancing%20%3D%201%20THEN%20total_volume%20ELSE%200%20END)%3A%3Adouble%20%2F%0A%20%20%20NULLIF(SUM(CASE%20WHEN%20is_declining%20%3D%201%20THEN%20total_volume%20ELSE%200%20END)%2C%200))%20AS%20trin%0AFROM%20classified%3B&executeQuery=true)

    WITH candles AS (  SELECT timestamp, symbol,    last(price) AS close_price,    sum(quantity) AS total_volume  FROM fx_trades  WHERE timestamp IN '$today'  SAMPLE BY 5m),with_previous AS (  SELECT timestamp, symbol, total_volume, close_price,    LAG(close_price) OVER (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp) AS last_close  FROM candles),classified AS (  SELECT timestamp, symbol, total_volume,    CASE WHEN close_price > last_close THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_advancing,    CASE WHEN close_price < last_close THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_declining  FROM with_previous  WHERE last_close IS NOT NULL)SELECT  timestamp,  SUM(is_advancing) AS advancing,  SUM(is_declining) AS declining,  SUM(CASE WHEN is_advancing = 1 THEN total_volume ELSE 0 END) AS advancing_volume,  SUM(CASE WHEN is_declining = 1 THEN total_volume ELSE 0 END) AS declining_volume,  (SUM(is_advancing)::double / NULLIF(SUM(is_declining), 0)) /  (SUM(CASE WHEN is_advancing = 1 THEN total_volume ELSE 0 END)::double /   NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN is_declining = 1 THEN total_volume ELSE 0 END), 0)) AS trinFROM classified;

### Interpreting TRIN[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#interpreting-trin "Direct link to Interpreting TRIN")

*   **< 1.0**: Volume favoring advances (bullish)
*   **\> 1.0**: Volume favoring declines (bearish)
*   **\= 1.0**: Neutral

note

TRIN can produce counterintuitive results. If advances outnumber declines 2:1 and advancing volume also leads 2:1, TRIN equals 1.0 (neutral) despite bullish conditions. Always consider TRIN alongside the raw advancing/declining counts.

Related documentation

*   [Window functions](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/syntax/)
    
*   [LAG function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/reference/#lag)
    
*   [SAMPLE BY](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/sample-by/)
    

*   [TICK](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#tick)
    *   [TICK snapshot](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#tick-snapshot)
        
    *   [Interpreting TICK](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#interpreting-tick)
        
*   [TRIN](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#trin)
    *   [TRIN snapshot](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#trin-snapshot)
        
    *   [TRIN time-series](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#trin-time-series)
        
    *   [Interpreting TRIN](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/tick-trin/#interpreting-trin)

# Misc Operators | QuestDB

On this page

`.` Prefix[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/misc/#-prefix "Direct link to -prefix")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The `.` operator is used to prefix columns with a table name, for example when performing joins.

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/misc/#example "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT *FROM a, bWHERE a.id = b.id;

`::` Cast[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/misc/#-cast "Direct link to -cast")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`::` performs Postgres-style casts.

We recommend the use of [`CAST`](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/cast/)
 instead of this operator.

This operator is returned for compatibility with Postgres syntax, so not all conversions will occur as you'd expect.

For example, `5::FLOAT` will return a `DOUBLE`, not a `FLOAT`.

#### Example[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/misc/#example-1 "Direct link to Example")

    SELECT 5::float, cast(5 as float)

| cast | cast1 |
| --- | --- |
| 5.0 | 5.0 |

*   [`.` Prefix](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/misc/#-prefix)
    
*   [`::` Cast](https://questdb.com/docs/query/operators/misc/#-cast)

---

# Keltner Channels | QuestDB

On this page

Keltner Channels are volatility-based bands set above and below an EMA. Unlike Bollinger Bands which use standard deviation, Keltner Channels use Average True Range (ATR), making them less sensitive to sudden price spikes.

Problem[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/keltner-channels/#problem "Direct link to Problem")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You want volatility bands that adapt to market conditions but are smoother than Bollinger Bands. Bollinger Bands can expand rapidly on single large moves, while Keltner Channels respond more gradually to sustained volatility changes.

Solution[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/keltner-channels/#solution "Direct link to Solution")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Calculate Keltner Channels (20-period EMA ± 2× ATR)[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=DECLARE%0A%20%20%40symbol%20%3A%3D%20%27EURUSD%27%2C%0A%20%20%40lookback%20%3A%3D%20%27%24now%20-%202d..%24now%27%0A%0AWITH%20with_prev%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20high%2C%0A%20%20%20%20low%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20lag(close)%20OVER%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp)%20AS%20prev_close%0A%20%20FROM%20market_data_ohlc_15m%0A%20%20WHERE%20symbol%20%3D%20%40symbol%0A%20%20%20%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%40lookback%0A)%2C%0Awith_tr%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20high%2C%0A%20%20%20%20low%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20greatest(%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20high%20-%20low%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20abs(high%20-%20prev_close)%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20abs(low%20-%20prev_close)%0A%20%20%20%20)%20AS%20tr%0A%20%20FROM%20with_prev%0A%20%20WHERE%20prev_close%20IS%20NOT%20NULL%0A)%2C%0Awith_indicators%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%0A%20%20%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20close%2C%0A%20%20%20%20avg(close%2C%20%27period%27%2C%2020)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20ema20%2C%0A%20%20%20%20avg(tr%2C%20%27period%27%2C%2020)%20OVER%20w%20AS%20atr%0A%20%20FROM%20with_tr%0A%20%20WINDOW%20w%20AS%20(PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%20ORDER%20BY%20timestamp)%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20round(close%2C%205)%20AS%20close%2C%0A%20%20round(ema20%2C%205)%20AS%20middle%2C%0A%20%20round(ema20%20%2B%202%20*%20atr%2C%205)%20AS%20upper%2C%0A%20%20round(ema20%20-%202%20*%20atr%2C%205)%20AS%20lower%0AFROM%20with_indicators%0AORDER%20BY%20timestamp%3B&executeQuery=true)

    DECLARE  @symbol := 'EURUSD',  @lookback := '$now - 2d..$now'WITH with_prev AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    high,    low,    close,    lag(close) OVER (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp) AS prev_close  FROM market_data_ohlc_15m  WHERE symbol = @symbol    AND timestamp IN @lookback),with_tr AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    high,    low,    close,    greatest(      high - low,      abs(high - prev_close),      abs(low - prev_close)    ) AS tr  FROM with_prev  WHERE prev_close IS NOT NULL),with_indicators AS (  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    close,    avg(close, 'period', 20) OVER w AS ema20,    avg(tr, 'period', 20) OVER w AS atr  FROM with_tr  WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY timestamp))SELECT  timestamp,  symbol,  round(close, 5) AS close,  round(ema20, 5) AS middle,  round(ema20 + 2 * atr, 5) AS upper,  round(ema20 - 2 * atr, 5) AS lowerFROM with_indicatorsORDER BY timestamp;

The query:

1.  Calculates True Range accounting for gaps
2.  Applies 20-period EMA smoothing to both price and ATR
3.  Creates bands at ±2 ATR from the EMA

Interpreting results[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/keltner-channels/#interpreting-results "Direct link to Interpreting results")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Price above upper**: Strong uptrend or overbought
*   **Price below lower**: Strong downtrend or oversold
*   **Price at middle (EMA)**: Mean reversion target
*   **Channels widening**: Volatility increasing
*   **Channels narrowing**: Volatility decreasing

Keltner squeeze[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/keltner-channels/#keltner-squeeze "Direct link to Keltner squeeze")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

When Bollinger Bands move inside Keltner Channels, it signals extremely low volatility (a "squeeze"). See the [Bollinger BandWidth recipe](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bandwidth/)
 for measuring squeeze conditions.

Related documentation

*   [Bollinger Bands](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/bollinger-bands/)
    
*   [ATR recipe](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/atr/)
    
*   [EMA window function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/window-functions/reference/#avg)
    

*   [Problem](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/keltner-channels/#problem)
    
*   [Solution](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/keltner-channels/#solution)
    
*   [Interpreting results](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/keltner-channels/#interpreting-results)
    
*   [Keltner squeeze](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/keltner-channels/#keltner-squeeze)

---

# Volume profile | QuestDB

On this page

Calculate volume profile to show the distribution of trading volume across different price levels.

Solution[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/volume-profile/#solution "Direct link to Solution")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Group trades into price bins using `FLOOR` and a tick size parameter:

Calculate volume profile with fixed tick size[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=DECLARE%20%40tick_size%20%3A%3D%200.01%0ASELECT%0A%20%20floor(price%20%2F%20%40tick_size)%20*%20%40tick_size%20AS%20price_bin%2C%0A%20%20round(SUM(quantity)%2C%202)%20AS%20volume%0AFROM%20fx_trades%0AWHERE%20symbol%20%3D%20%27EURUSD%27%0A%20%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%27%24today%27%0AORDER%20BY%20price_bin%3B&executeQuery=true)

    DECLARE @tick_size := 0.01SELECT  floor(price / @tick_size) * @tick_size AS price_bin,  round(SUM(quantity), 2) AS volumeFROM fx_tradesWHERE symbol = 'EURUSD'  AND timestamp IN '$today'ORDER BY price_bin;

Since QuestDB does an implicit GROUP BY on all non-aggregated columns, you can omit the explicit GROUP BY clause.

Dynamic tick size[​](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/volume-profile/#dynamic-tick-size "Direct link to Dynamic tick size")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

For consistent histograms across different price ranges, calculate the tick size dynamically to always produce approximately 50 bins:

Volume profile with dynamic 50-bin distribution[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=WITH%20raw_data%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%20price%2C%20quantity%0A%20%20FROM%20fx_trades%0A%20%20WHERE%20symbol%20%3D%20%27EURUSD%27%20AND%20timestamp%20IN%20%27%24today%27%0A)%2C%0Atick_size%20AS%20(%0A%20%20SELECT%20(max(price)%20-%20min(price))%20%2F%2049%20as%20tick_size%0A%20%20FROM%20raw_data%0A)%0ASELECT%0A%20%20floor(price%20%2F%20tick_size)%20*%20tick_size%20AS%20price_bin%2C%0A%20%20round(SUM(quantity)%2C%202)%20AS%20volume%0AFROM%20raw_data%20CROSS%20JOIN%20tick_size%0AORDER%20BY%201%3B&executeQuery=true)

    WITH raw_data AS (  SELECT price, quantity  FROM fx_trades  WHERE symbol = 'EURUSD' AND timestamp IN '$today'),tick_size AS (  SELECT (max(price) - min(price)) / 49 as tick_size  FROM raw_data)SELECT  floor(price / tick_size) * tick_size AS price_bin,  round(SUM(quantity), 2) AS volumeFROM raw_data CROSS JOIN tick_sizeORDER BY 1;

This will produce a histogram with a maximum of 50 buckets. If you have enough price difference between the first and last price for the interval, and if there are enough events with different prices, then you will get the full 50 buckets. If price difference is too small or if there are buckets with no events, then you might get less than 50.

Related Documentation

*   [FLOOR function](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/numeric/#floor)
    
*   [SUM aggregate](https://questdb.com/docs/query/functions/aggregation/#sum)
    
*   [DECLARE variables](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/declare/)
    
*   [CROSS JOIN](https://questdb.com/docs/query/sql/join/#cross-join)
    

*   [Solution](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/volume-profile/#solution)
    
*   [Dynamic tick size](https://questdb.com/docs/cookbook/sql/finance/volume-profile/#dynamic-tick-size)

---

# R PGwire Guide | QuestDB

On this page

QuestDB is tested with the following R client:

*   [RPostgres](https://rpostgres.r-dbi.org/)
     with [DBI](https://dbi.r-dbi.org/)
    

Other R clients that are compatible with the PostgreSQL wire protocol should also work with QuestDB, but we do not test them. If you find a client that does not work, please [open an issue](https://github.com/questdb/questdb/issues/new?template=bug_report.yaml)
.

### Performance Considerations[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#performance-considerations "Direct link to Performance Considerations")

QuestDB is a high-performance database. The PGWire protocol has many flavors, and some of them are not optimized for performance. For best performance when querying data from QuestDB with R, we recommend using RPostgres with the DBI interface.

tip

For data ingestion, we recommend using QuestDB's first-party clients with the [InfluxDB Line Protocol (ILP)](https://questdb.com/docs/ingestion/overview/)
 instead of PGWire. PGWire should primarily be used for querying data in QuestDB.

Introduction to PGWire in QuestDB[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#introduction-to-pgwire-in-questdb "Direct link to Introduction to PGWire in QuestDB")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

QuestDB supports the PostgreSQL Wire Protocol (PGWire) for querying data. This compatibility allows you to use standard R PostgreSQL clients with QuestDB's high-performance time-series database.

It's important to note that QuestDB's underlying storage model differs from PostgreSQL's, which means some PostgreSQL features may not be available in QuestDB.

Connection Parameters[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#connection-parameters "Direct link to Connection Parameters")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The RPostgres client needs the following connection parameters to connect to QuestDB:

*   **host**: The hostname or IP address of the QuestDB server (default: `localhost`)
*   **port**: The PostgreSQL wire protocol port (default: `8812`)
*   **user**: The username for authentication (default: `admin`)
*   **password**: The password for authentication (default: `quest`)
*   **dbname**: The database name (default: `qdb`)

RPostgres with DBI[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#rpostgres-with-dbi "Direct link to RPostgres with DBI")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[RPostgres](https://rpostgres.r-dbi.org/)
 is a modern DBI-compliant database backend for R that connects to PostgreSQL. It uses the DBI interface for a consistent workflow with other database types in R.

### Features[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#features "Direct link to Features")

*   DBI compliance for consistent database programming in R
*   Support for parameterized queries

### Installation[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#installation "Direct link to Installation")

Install the required packages from CRAN:

    install.packages(c("RPostgres", "DBI"), repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")

### Basic Connection[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#basic-connection "Direct link to Basic Connection")

Here's a basic example of connecting to QuestDB using RPostgres:

    library(RPostgres)library(DBI)con <- dbConnect(  Redshift(),  dbname = "qdb",  host = "localhost",  port = 8812,  user = "admin",  password = "quest")if (dbIsValid(con)) {  cat("Successfully connected to QuestDB!\n")  version <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT version()")  print(version)  dbDisconnect(con)} else {  cat("Failed to connect to QuestDB.\n")}

note

When connecting to QuestDB with RPostgres, use `Redshift()` instead of `Postgres()` as the connection method. QuestDB implements a subset of the PostgreSQL wire protocol similar to Amazon Redshift. Using the `Redshift()` configuration instructs RPostgres to avoid PostgreSQL-specific features that QuestDB doesn't support, improving compatibility.

### Querying Data[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#querying-data "Direct link to Querying Data")

RPostgres with DBI provides several functions for executing queries:

    library(RPostgres)library(DBI)# Connect to QuestDBcon <- dbConnect(  Redshift(),  dbname = "qdb",  host = "localhost",  port = 8812,  user = "admin",  password = "quest")trades <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM public.trades LIMIT 10")print(trades)# Close the connectiondbDisconnect(con)

### Parameterized Queries[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#parameterized-queries "Direct link to Parameterized Queries")

Using parameterized queries helps prevent SQL injection and improves code readability:

    library(RPostgres)library(DBI)con <- dbConnect(  Redshift(),  dbname = "qdb",  host = "localhost",  port = 8812,  user = "admin",  password = "quest")symbol <- "BTC-USD"limit_rows <- 10# Method 1: Using parameter substitution (safest approach)query <- "SELECT * FROM trades WHERE symbol = $1 ORDER BY timestamp DESC LIMIT $2"trades <- dbGetQuery(con, query, params = list(symbol, limit_rows))print(trades)# Method 2: Using glue_sql from glue package (if installed)if (requireNamespace("glue", quietly = TRUE)) {  library(glue)  query <- glue_sql("SELECT * FROM trades WHERE symbol = {symbol} ORDER BY timestamp DESC LIMIT {as.integer(limit_rows)}",                    .con = con)  trades2 <- dbGetQuery(con, query)  print(trades2)}# Close the connectiondbDisconnect(con)

### Handling QuestDB-Specific Time-Series Queries[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#handling-questdb-specific-time-series-queries "Direct link to Handling QuestDB-Specific Time-Series Queries")

QuestDB provides specialized time-series functions that can be used with RPostgres:

    library(RPostgres)library(DBI)# Connect to QuestDBcon <- dbConnect(  Redshift(),  dbname = "qdb",  host = "localhost",  port = 8812,  user = "admin",  password = "quest")# SAMPLE BY query (time-based downsampling)cat("Executing SAMPLE BY query...\n")sampled_data <- dbGetQuery(con, "  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    avg(price) as avg_price,    min(price) as min_price,    max(price) as max_price  FROM trades  WHERE timestamp >= dateadd('d', -7000, now())  SAMPLE BY 1h")print(head(sampled_data))# LATEST ON query (last value per group)cat("\nExecuting LATEST ON query...\n")latest_data <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM trades LATEST ON timestamp PARTITION BY symbol")print(latest_data)# Close the connectiondbDisconnect(con)

### Integration with Popular R Packages[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#integration-with-popular-r-packages "Direct link to Integration with Popular R Packages")

Here's how to integrate QuestDB with popular R analysis packages:

    library(RPostgres)library(DBI)library(dplyr)library(ggplot2)# Connect to QuestDBcon <- dbConnect(  Redshift(),  dbname = "qdb",  host = "localhost",  port = 8812,  user = "admin",  password = "quest")# Fetch hourly sampled price datahourly_prices <- dbGetQuery(con, "  SELECT    timestamp,    symbol,    avg(price) as avg_price  FROM trades  WHERE timestamp >= dateadd('d', -30, now())    AND symbol IN ('BTC-USD', 'ETH-USD')  SAMPLE BY 1h")# Process data with dplyrprocessed_data <- hourly_prices %>%  mutate(    date = as_date(timestamp),    hour = hour(timestamp)  ) %>%  group_by(symbol, date) %>%  summarize(    daily_avg = mean(avg_price),    daily_min = min(avg_price),    daily_max = max(avg_price),    volatility = daily_max - daily_min,    .groups = 'drop'  )print(head(processed_data))# Create a plot with ggplot2p <- ggplot(hourly_prices, aes(x = timestamp, y = avg_price, color = symbol)) +  geom_line() +  labs(    title = "Cryptocurrency Prices - 30 Day History",    x = "Date",    y = "Price (USD)",    color = "Symbol"  ) +  theme_minimal()print(p)# Close the connectiondbDisconnect(con)

### Known Limitations with QuestDB[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#known-limitations-with-questdb "Direct link to Known Limitations with QuestDB")

When using RPostgres with QuestDB, be aware of these limitations:

1.  **Transaction Semantics**: QuestDB has different transaction semantics compared to traditional RDBMS.
2.  **Schema Management**: QuestDB's table creation and schema modification capabilities differ from PostgreSQL.
3.  **R-Specific Data Types**: Some R data types might not map directly to QuestDB types.
4.  **Time Zone Handling**: Be careful with time zone conversions, as R and QuestDB might handle them differently.

### Performance Tips[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#performance-tips "Direct link to Performance Tips")

1.  **Use RPostgres**: RPostgres is generally faster than older R PostgreSQL drivers.
2.  **Limit Result Sets**: When working with large datasets, use LIMIT clauses to avoid memory issues in R.
3.  **Parameterized Queries**: Use parameterized queries for better security and performance.
4.  **Optimize Queries**: Take advantage of QuestDB's time-series functions like `SAMPLE BY` and `LATEST ON` for efficient queries.
5.  **Pre-filter Data**: Perform filtering in SQL rather than in R when possible.

QuestDB Time Series Features[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#questdb-time-series-features "Direct link to QuestDB Time Series Features")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

QuestDB provides specialized time-series functions that can be used with RPostgres:

### SAMPLE BY Queries[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#sample-by-queries "Direct link to SAMPLE BY Queries")

SAMPLE BY is used for time-based downsampling:

Sample By 1 Hour[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=SELECT%20timestamp%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20avg(price)%20as%20avg_price%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20min(price)%20as%20min_price%2C%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20max(price)%20as%20max_price%0AFROM%20trades%0AWHERE%20timestamp%20%3E%3D%20dateadd(%27d%27%2C%20-7%2C%20now())%20SAMPLE%20BY%201h%3B&executeQuery=true)

    SELECT timestamp,       symbol,       avg(price) as avg_price,       min(price) as min_price,       max(price) as max_priceFROM tradesWHERE timestamp >= dateadd('d', -7, now()) SAMPLE BY 1h;

### LATEST ON Queries[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#latest-on-queries "Direct link to LATEST ON Queries")

LATEST ON is an efficient way to get the most recent values:

LATEST Rows Per Symbol[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=SELECT%20*%0AFROM%20trades%0AWHERE%20timestamp%20IN%20today()%0ALATEST%20ON%20timestamp%20PARTITION%20BY%20symbol%3B&executeQuery=true)

    SELECT *FROM tradesWHERE timestamp IN today()LATEST ON timestamp PARTITION BY symbol;

Highly-Available Reads with QuestDB Enterprise[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#highly-available-reads-with-questdb-enterprise "Direct link to Highly-Available Reads with QuestDB Enterprise")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

QuestDB Enterprise supports running [multiple replicas](https://questdb.com/docs/high-availability/setup/)
 to serve queries. Client applications can specify **multiple hosts** in the connection string. This ensures that initial connections succeed even if a node is down. If the connected node fails later, the application should catch the error, reconnect to another host, and retry the read.

See our blog post for background and the companion repository for a minimal example:

*   Blog: [Highly-available reads with QuestDB](https://questdb.com/blog/highly-available-reads-with-questdb/)
    
*   Example: [questdb/questdb-ha-reads](https://github.com/questdb/questdb-ha-reads)
    

PGWire Known Limitations with QuestDB[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#pgwire-known-limitations-with-questdb "Direct link to PGWire Known Limitations with QuestDB")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Some PostgreSQL-specific features (complex transaction semantics, exotic data types, certain metadata calls) may not be fully supported.
*   Cursors/scrollable result sets and some ORM expectations may behave differently than in PostgreSQL.
*   Prefer **querying** via PGWire and **ingestion** via ILP for best throughput.

Troubleshooting[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#troubleshooting "Direct link to Troubleshooting")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Connection issues[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#connection-issues "Direct link to Connection issues")

1.  Verify QuestDB is running and listening on port **8812**.
2.  Check credentials and network access.
3.  Try a minimal query: `SELECT 1`.
4.  Inspect QuestDB server logs for connection or auth errors.

### Query Errors[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#query-errors "Direct link to Query Errors")

For query-related errors:

1.  Verify that the table you're querying exists
2.  Check the syntax of your SQL query
3.  Ensure that you're using the correct data types for parameters
4.  Look for any unsupported PostgreSQL features that might be causing issues

### Timestamp confusion[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#timestamp-confusion "Direct link to Timestamp confusion")

*   Remember: **QuestDB stores and encodes timestamps always as UTC**.

### Data Type Issues[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#data-type-issues "Direct link to Data Type Issues")

For data type-related problems:

1.  Use `str()` to check the structure of your R data frames.
2.  Ensure date and timestamp columns are properly converted using `as.POSIXct()`.
3.  For numeric columns, verify that values are not being converted to characters.

Conclusion[​](https://questdb.com/docs/query/pgwire/r/#conclusion "Direct link to Conclusion")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

RPostgres with DBI provides a robust way to connect R applications to QuestDB through the PostgreSQL Wire Protocol. By following the guidelines in this documentation, you can effectively query time-series data from QuestDB and integrate it with R's powerful data analysis and visualization capabilities.

For data ingestion, it's recommended to use QuestDB's first-party clients with the InfluxDB Line Protocol (ILP) for high-throughput data insertion.

QuestDB's SQL extensions for time-series data, such as `SAMPLE BY` and `LATEST ON`, provide powerful tools for analyzing time-series data that can be easily accessed through R.
